Comment: A big source of dissatisfaction within my role (Assistant Psychologist) is the poor pay and the lack of options for progression.  There are few options for me to progress which I will be likely to get on to (i.e. the doctorate), which combined with the poor pay and challenging nature of the work, is not a good mix for my mental health.
A four day week would be help with health and well-being allowing more family time and reduce burnout caused by stress and illness.  Offering longer shifts in certain services instead of usual 9-5.

Admin staff are being forced to work in the office full time.  Admin are told it doesn't work from home without explanation, it is not true and many admin staff can do their entire job remotely clinical staff are treated better and are allowed to work remotely when they can.  Admin staff are being treated unfairly and do not get the respect they deserve.

All my negative comments are the higher management, don't feel apricated, they don't support our team if it wasn't for my colleges in work I would of left years ago, I got diagnosed with [conditions removed] on [date removed].  And heard from no one.

Although I thoroughly enjoy being a Nurse.  I feel this role has took it's toll on me emotionally and physically.  I did not go into the profession with the view to earn a lot of money, but to help people.  However the work increased work demands, and the added pressures, increases anxiety that I could do something wrong, forget something, carry out a drug error.  There is no amount of money that would alleviate this feeling.  Furthermore, I have missed Christmas and birthdays with my [family members removed].  I haven't been on a holiday abroad with my [family members removed] in over 12 years due to financial restraints and not being able to book A/L when the [family members removed] are available due to staffing issues.  I am looking to change my career and leave nursing within the near future.  I have not made this decision lightly, questioning myself and looked at the roles I am in, blaming COVID 19 for my feelings.  Reflecting now, its the role as a Nurse that its what has been the issue and made me unhappy.

Although this is a great organisation to work for, I do feel many staff abuse the good nature and support of Mersey care as it is a 'Mental Health Advocate'.  I feel HR and decision making on staff is too lenient when it comes to sickness, absence and performance.  Other organisations have trial periods for staff to 'prove' they can do what they confirmed at interview.  Here, as long as you pass the interview, even if poor at your job, you have a job for life.  I work with staff who are on decent pay but play the fool or blame mental health on their performance which is accepted.  They are allowed to continue on low input when others have to work harder to accommodate them and I feel management have their hands tied as HR and Occupational Health support the individual more instead of appreciating the impact on the department/service that the staff member works for.  The organisation and NHS is struggling to provide a great service for patients as we are too busy dealing with problematic staff.  Absence and sickness is too lenient, staff are staged but don't seem to care as staging leads to nothing.  In other organisations the processes in place made you think twice about how bad your record would look having too much sickness and absence and equally you felt bad letting your colleagues down.  Mersey care allow poor attendees and performers to move freely from one department to another with no responsibility.  I feel we are very lucky with the privileges we have working for this organisation as I have worked for some terrible and strict employers and it angers me that with the state the NHS is in, that we have staff who think the organisation owes them a better life and spend more time not working than working and complain.  They have either never worked for another organisation, or forgotten how lucky we are.
An NHS budget is a guide, management should concentrate on staff morale as without good staff your never going to hit any target.

As a department it is a fact we are short staffed.  And have a exodus of clinicians this year.  While the rest of us perform at our AFC band it is demoralising to see that clinicians are brought in as band 7 AFC and doing band 5-6 work as it was the only way anyone will apply.  Within the profession we have a bad name of over working staff.  The staff that remain are loyal to each other and that is the main reason they are in work.  To have staff in tears daily on a team meeting and stating its too much, I cant cope, stop adding to my case load is heart breaking.  We have manager who is living in the past and how thing's worked where she used to work.  She needs to be in the moment and face with the issues here and now.  Managers saying they see patients in their unpaid lunch, isn't a solution and should be condoned.  As this is then a sign to lower staff members well if manager doing it I must do it as well.  If we need to see patients during unpaid lunch then logistically something requires addressing as just sticking a band aid over a issue doesn't resolve it.  We now see that advertised are positions higher than my pay grade where experience isn't necessary.  So leave they have just qualified from university.  No NHS experience, patient experiences or computer system skills and they are rewarded with a Band 6, a position unobtainable to me after [number removed] years service.  We have band 7's who just seem to spend time in meetings and admin.  In the past three years we have had managers who I personally feel haven't been equipped to do the job, very nice people but not management material.  I feel we are a department lost at sea, bobbing around on a ocean like a rudderless ship.  As AHP we have a duty to maintain our development with CPD.  As I am employed by the NHS this is a mutual arrangement where this essential training to maintain my professionalism is given by the trust.  Continually meeting after meeting and CPD session after CPD session has been cancelled last minute due to staff shortages.  Again this bring despondency to the team.  We get no down time or time to catch up with each other.  As clinicians we work autonomously so we look forward to these sessions.  Our commitment to patients?  We have patients adults and children in pain with acute conditions,  are we being open and honest and writing and telling them just how long they are going to have to wait to have the treatment they are waiting for?

As a whole I feel that we as a team are feeling generally burnt out and that morale is at an all time low- we are currently severely understaffed, operating on EMS level 3 for a long period now with an ever increasing workload which we are expected to complete within an unrealistic time frame.  This in turn is causing many staff to take long term sickness due to work related stress and this is further exacerbating the problem even with aid from other trusts etc.  This has been ongoing now for several months and does not seem to be getting any better and I am concerned about the further implications of this on the team in general.  I would however like to say a massive thank you to the team managers who are doing an excellent job of showing their support towards us as a team during these difficult times and especially towards myself- I am currently going through a lot of [details removed] issues and management have been incredibly supportive towards me with everything from checking in on me regularly to accommodating appointments which I have needed to attend and ensuring I know that they are only a phone call away if needed- in fact I would like to offer my most recent thanks to my team manager for taking time out of there day off to call me after I had emailed to express my levels of work related stress and how this has been affecting me as I really do feel this was above and beyond the call of duty and I would like this to be recognised higher up.  Thank you!

As an admin assistant I feel totally indispensable at the moment.  Events have occurred which although they are being looked into I feel this always comes down to cost rather than staff safety.  Far too many managers who seem to give conflicting replies to queries and admin not being replaced making work much more stressful.  Expected to cover a wide variety of duties in different venues so trying to split myself in too many different directions.  I am totally disenchanted with my role at the moment and can't see this improving any time soon.

As I have only been in post for a [timeframe removed] I feel I cant make too many comments on this organisation.  But what I have seen up to now of my team and the organisation so far I cant complain.
At present I am happy in my job.

Awful communications in this organisation.  Bullying senior leadership teams led by narcissist sociopaths with dunning kruger tendencies.  Plenty of nepotism for all levels of senior management make this a truly toxic place to work.  Mangers have fantastic ideas on running services without the faintest real world insight into how they might operate, and fail to listen to staff who have a wealth more knowledge than them.  Operational managers have a deep suspicion of their staff and this manifests in a culture of expecting failure and punishing innovation.  Flexible working offer is lip service only.  Nice way to treat the Whalley grange staff, that's how this place operates.

Band 2 staff that are not just doing (what is described in their role for Band 2), so doing extra in their roles and responsibility's should be on a Band 3.  Getting information from hospitals, having to collect sickle cell results and booking sickle blood tests etc should surely be on a Band 3 as this is more intense but vital for the team and especially the patient.

Better trust wide support for disabled staff Is desperately needed.  Trust wide training for all staff regarding neurodiversity's, including information regarding support, understanding and acceptance for any colleagues diagnosed with a neurodiverse condition is required.  Better timely organisation of reasonable adjustments support implementation, and more better action from the trust in regards to ensuring access to work awarded support is ordered and implemented in a timely manner by the trust is very much needed.  More stronger visibility and visible leadership from senior management and senior HR is needed in relation to support for disabled staff.  Maybe senior managers (CEO and the executive team) can consider having a bi monthly alternative virtual drop in session for disabled staff that is not the same as the staff disability network meetings but is led by the CEO, chief nurse and other senior managers.  Having this in place would be beneficial to the trust to show strong leadership that disabled staff will be better supported in future, and that there is an open forum for disabled staff to feel that their concerns, ideas and needs are being listened to and taken on board.  This will also help disabled staff to not be discriminated against, and to ensure that they will not face any discrimination in relation to their reasonable adjustments or because of the simple fact they require reasonable adjustments.  The proposed business case for the reasonable adjustments officer roles needs to be heard as soon as possible to ensure that hopefully this role can be implemented to better support the current and future disabled workforce, and improve experiences for disabled colleagues, managers and other trust wide colleagues.

Brilliant team, which all help each other and strive for excellence/best practice.  Amazing, caring and supportive manager and band 6 supervisors, who are always there to help.  Very happy in my place of work.
Brilliant trust to work for.
Bullies get looked after.
Bullying and nepotism is rife.

Bullying culture evident at senior level.  Senior managers not upholding trust values.  Staff are not being valued or recognised for genuine hard work and the expertise and value they bring.  Some staff given unfair advantage due to who they know.

Career progression is limited because it is not offered to all, just those that tick the right boxes or do certain roles.  Changing your role or seeking training whilst supporting a family is extremely difficult.

Certain members of SLT due to their relationship with execs display inappropriate behaviour to other members of the SLT which goes unchallenged by the chair.  Staff have raised concerns about nepotism and how their behaviour is accepted which is upsetting to see when it badly affects senior staff.  Work that would allow staff to develop is given straight to certain staff, again due to relationships, and excludes other staff members preventing their development.  Civility and respect does not include these 2 senior leaders they speak and behave however they like with lack of challenge.

Clinical staff need to have the opportunity to work from home to complete admin tasks as much as the managing staff do.  Also mental health days should be brought in to stop people leaving the role within the first 2 years.
Comments regarding my experience with 1 manager were addressed by myself during my PACE and discussed.  Staff witnessed and raised it within the team.
Communication in all areas need to improve.

Community nursing has changed dramatically since the 'Waiting' for patients was originally designed.  For example - having a waiting of 1 so 15 minutes for an insulin visit is ridiculous when it takes 20 minutes to even drive to the patient.  Patient's are more complex and we have taken on more responsibilities but the time for this is remains the same.  We are continuously working through our unpaid lunch time and into the evening when we are at home.  Also there needs to be better communication between the hospitals and the community as we often get no referral for a patient or a limited one that lacks important information.

Considering how many staff the team is short by, it would be nice to bring back overtime without having to have it approved at the last second when you're absolutely desperate for staff.  Also, when someone speaks up against bullying, it would be nice if those who then speak up as a result, are not treated like criminals and told by the 8B not to gossip.  Not really sure how Mersey Care has managed to miss the ball so terribly with freedom to speak up.  Usually when one person whistle blows, it causes a domino effect of other people finding the confidence to speak up.  When this happened in my team, concerns fell upon deaf ears and we were told it was a 'witch hunt' and the person accused was protected and not investigated.  The original person who was spoken up about has now left and 10 staff members have left since person accused.  Maybe you should take a look at that.

Considering the trust has a reported learning culture, can I suggest that bands 8 and above consider this as these people in the hierarchy need to wake up and pull their fingers out!  The only group of people learning are the clinicians at the sharp end?  Glad to be retiring within the next year as I find Mersey care absolutely embarrassing at board and higher levels - not a clue!

Continually bombarded with emails and information.  I feel that half the job is taking up with satisfying policies and procedures which leaves less time for clinical practice.  This is not always reflected when scrutinizing time allocation between clinical and no-clinical duties.  More time is spent using IT which at times connectivity is an issue and my eyes sight has deteriorated due to the amount of screen time now expected as part of carrying out my job role.
Currently happy in my role.

Currently I am clear on what my responsibilities are, although this can change sometimes.  I feel because my role is split, Senior Admin Support and Admin Team Leader, I cannot dedicate my time fully to both.  When applying and accepting my role, it was advertised as "Senior Administrator", and this is what is on my Employment Contract.  I do not feel it was clear that my role would be split.  My Job Description states no mention of managing staff within different teams or different localities.  I currently manage six staff across Liverpool and Sefton Place.

Currently[timeframe and condition removed] and totally anxious.  I have[details removed] previous and this is my [details removed] and first baby.  I am only mentioning this because I can't thank my manager enough for the help and support they have given me in returning to work from a period of morning sickness and constant worry/anxiety around this pregnancy.  Occupational health  was also amazing and really helpful but my manager has been amazing.

Decision makers need to listen to clinicians rather than just say ridiculous comments like patients can book their own appointments online as their [family member removed] can do it and they are [age removed]!

Difficult survey to complete due to situation of admin staff being under an admin management hub and no longer under the management of the team they work for - this is causing frustration, resentment, stress, and feelings of being under valued.  Removal of admin staff from their clinical colleagues in services where they work together closely is causing admin staff to feel demotivated, disengaged and separated from their clinical counterparts and the collective ethos of the service.  It is also disorganised, inefficient, impractical and frustrating way to work.  Decisions and opinions are made externally without the input of the staff from the service either clinical or admin which has bearing on the operational running of the department but without any knowledge - leaving staff to pick up the pieces and seek out work around solutions which is time wasting and exhausting.  Admin staff have also now been denied the opportunities of flexible working and told must be office based 5 days a week regardless of job role - when seeking clarity over this have been told that it does not work and it is 'the directive from above' - which has caused feeling of resentment, undermining and under-valued.

District nursing is for housebound patients but staff think it would be helpful for my organisation to employ doctors for staff to seek medical advice when unwell as they may not get to see or have available appointments the same day.

Due to management restructure and an obsessive focus on KPIs, staff at every grade are feeling more and more pressures on their time.  Band 7's are being forced to spend more time on non-clinical activities pushing pressure further down the line.  Staff morale is low, good staff are leaving due to this.  Staff also leaving due to no career progression opportunities as Band 7 roles which are being vacated are not being replaced.  It doesn't really feel like a nice place to work anymore.

Earlier this year l asked if l could retire and return as l had given[timeframe removed] of service to the NHS.  This was refused on the grounds of needing staff to cover the hours that l worked.  This was particularly irritating to me because of a number of staff in the team who have been allowed to retire and return.  These are all trained staff who been awarded the consideration of retiring and returning.  l feel l have not been awarded the same consideration as the trained staff in the team.  Very ageist decision as far as l am concerned.  General staff feel under pressure and under valued by managerial staff particularly the grade 8 staff who are neither use nor ornament to the real workers.
Easier access to equipment when faulty such as laptops.  Manager is very helpful and supportive - Good practice is encouraged fully.  More adequate staffing.

Excessive and increasing workload - not achievable, increasing stress levels and staff morale very low, staff regularly working through lunch and after hours.  Feeling micro-managed rather than supported.  KPI focused.  Documentation exorbitant and repetitive.  Not feeling listened to.  Clerical support has declined since clerks are not based in offices.
Fantastic supportive manager absolutely divine role.

Far too many new ways of working/pilots introduced into teams.  Unsafe to introduce so many pilots together.  Staffing levels are not reflected/increased when extra work is put on to teams.  Very poor visible presence from Senior Leads 8B and above, despite requests for support from Teams.  Definitely looking to move elsewhere within the NHS.

Feel that service mergers/changes for example for services now covering wider catchment area there is reduced quality of service/more pressure on clinical staff/room for error e.g.  For cases whereby they have not seen the same member of staff more than once for follow-up reviews especially in urgent care community setting.  Overall, feel that there is uncertainty regarding roles/service changes which impacts on staff morale and wellbeing and has meant that staff have moved roles due to this causing more pressure on staff left within teams.

Feel under valued, under paid and under appreciated in our roles.  We all need to be taught the basics BSL which has been asked numerous times throughout the years especially COVID times as we were wearing masks and removing them to speak, its common courtesy.  There are a lot of flaws in this system.

Feel well supported by my line manager and in my immediate team.  However do not feel well supported by higher management that has been reduced so that they cover more teams than previously.  Can see that more is being put onto the Band 7 level of staff adding to their stress and reducing their clinical time with patients.  Band 7 opportunities are being lost as when a band 7 job becomes available it is being down graded to band 6.  This has already resulted in the loss of a fabulous Band 6 clinician that has left the organisation due to being told there will be no opportunity for her to progress.  Feel that the skills, expertise and loyalty of experienced band 6 staff is very much being taken for granted.  We are expected to the bulk of clinical visits but also more being asked of us as more demands made on Band 7 time.

Flexible working is not available within our team unless that means wanting to reduce working days or hours.  Working from home is not an option unless you have an appointment it is not built into our working lives to help us have a better work life balance as mentioned in the flexible and hybrid working policy.
For a primarily mental heath organisation.  Your attitude towards staff with mental health problems is very poor.
Forever grateful for my consistent support/understanding from my Team Leader while navigating my [family member and details removed].

Found support from HR/Pensions when enquiring about possibility of secondment role impacting special class status very lacking.  Therefore did not pursue the secondment which would have given me chance to further develop management skills and experience.

Freedom to speak up -what a joke ....  Serious patient safety concerns due to incompetent negligent fellow [job title removed].  Wider employing Trust has zero idea how to deal with this issue.  Swept under the carpet for the 3rd time after 3rd NHS England /Practitioner Performance investigation.  [job title removed] in question still in post 2 years later albeit excluded from [details removed] work while my employing Trust 'manage' the situation.  Meanwhile the whistleblowers including me in my dept, are harassed and bullied by the wider Trust and subject to frivolous malicious grievance procedures; the outcome of which bear no relation to what actually happened and how and why many patients were harmed is ignored.  Several [job title removed] in my dept significantly affected by the wider Trust's mismanagement of this situation and had time off sick with workplace related stress as a consequence.  This further affects the ability of my dept to provide care for vulnerable referred patients.  Disillusioned at the moment and certainly view the past 2 years in my NHS clinical role as the worst in my [timeframe removed] career as a UK salaried Community dentist.

From a reports perspective, i.e. rapid reviews/ radars/ documenting in prep for QRV/ documenting safety huddles etc., I feel there has been a huge increase in this admin type work which takes time and has resulted in reducing clinical time to see patients and support staff.  This needs to be considered in respect of staffing.  There are tasks such as documenting MCA/ BI and completing shared decision tools which take a great deal of time and is not reflected in out staffs timetabling.  Staff are mostly clinical with little admin time to complete such tasks.  This needs to be addressed.
Great leadership team who are supportive.
Haven't had any management 1-1 supervision in Sefton in [timeframe removed] with regard to caseload management or health and wellbeing.
Having my occupation listed as AHP other says it all really about how valued I feel my profession is within this organisation.

Having worked in the organisation for the whole of my adult life.  I have always worked in Mental health division.  Had I have still been there my answers would be completely different.  The culture is Mental health division is shocking.  Since moving to [location and timeframe removed] ago I feel happy going into work to do a job I love supporting patients and families making a huge difference in people lives.  Had I not have left Mental health division I would have left to go to private sector.  I now have a good work life balance and I'm supported to do so.
Healthier food options at Hollins Park please, salad is not often available, cooked meals are fine but too heavy for lunchtime, need lighter options with plenty of fresh salad available.

Higher management need to understand and support us nurses more, we don't get dinner breaks ever as there's to much work put on us, as they say you us take your breaks its physically impossible.  Some days eat on go or don't eat at all, take work home with us, as there never enough staff are patient lists always high.  If mistakes are made high management come down on us, not understanding how hard day in day out we work.  The pressure is on us every day.  Its physically and mentally draining, they wonder why so much long term sick!  .  Things need to change for everyone's well being.  I love my job, but the stress of it is getting worse not better, exhausted all the time.

Honestly speaking I feel the comments or negative thoughts would not be addressed in surveys as I feel this exercise is a tick box.  The information that is collated never gets fed back to staff delivering the care.  As if that was the case here would be changes in the organisation.  As staff have approached myself about being frustrated regarding management and have been honest completing this survey.

How come this survey is supposed to be anonymous, yet I continually receive notifications to complete the survey?  It's a joke, the same as the Organisation as a whole.  Lowly Clerks are the lowest of the low.  I would never suggest anyone work for this organisation and I cannot wait to leave.
HR backing staff that shouldn't be here makes me feel less motivated in my role.  Clear breaches of conduct but ignored due to "Just and Learning Culture".
HR policies are not always followed in a timely manner leading to stress and confusion.
Hybrid model of working is life changing and provides a much better balance.  The option for people to decide where they work best and can output the most work is invaluable.

I am aware of the intense pressure the DN Staff work under, and this is reflected in our RADAR reporting system, incident that could be avoided, and have seen Nursing staff worn out, burnt out and tearful due to lack of staff and increased workloads with patients increasing complexity in the Community.  No time for staff to complete Training, new nurses taking on patients and increasing workloads whilst in preceptorship.

I am concerned that the senior leadership team are not visible.  The SLT apply/enforce working practices that are not realistic, they appear to only have concerns about stakeholders and appear to have no concerns for patient or staff well being.
I am currently a Band 3 support worker and feel that a lot of the work that I do, should be Band 4 because of the level of responsibility.

I am dismayed at how the changes within my organisation make it it clear that they do not care about its employees.  I have never worked in a company that does not care about a employees mental well being in particular.  Senior managers of my organisation have no clue about what happens on the ground nor do they wish to.  Its all about meeting their individual targets regardless of what measures are required to achieve them.
I am due to leave the organisation, I value the time I have worked here and hope to return in the future.

I am experiencing difficulties completing the RADAR system of reporting safety concerns.  The online form isn't fit for purpose.  The trust has become so large that forms are required to cover in patient, community, mental health, and physical health settings which makes forms such as RADAR very difficult to complete.  As a [details removed] I have concerns that errors /concerns aren't being recorded as staff when completing RADAR find the form isn't saved.
I am happy in my new role.  It is new and challenging but I am supported by the operational lead.

I am happy in my role I feel very supported by my immediate manager and the team I work with.  I believe there are too many management roles within the NHS, and since the transformation, clinical pathways are more confusing than ever.  Systems such as booking courses are overly complicated for some staff.  Systems should be user friendly.  In an IAPT service, whilst we are working with measures that we all agree are not fit for purpose, but will not change, has the potential to impact on staff morale and make attracting new staff difficult.
I am happy to work in my team, inside the organisation.  Many thanks.
I am happy with in my current role.

I am happy within my role at Merseycare however I feel there needs to be a stronger more effective process when involving absence.  I find there is little or no support from HR in these matters and seems to be no consequence for staff who have extensive sickness.  I spend a lot of my time dealing with team members absence instead of using my time for more development of staff that seem to want to be here, give back and have strong team values.
I am on a fixed term contract which is why I am looking for other roles.
I am proud of the team I work with and have very supportive colleagues and manager.  I'm also proud of the work we do for our families.

I am quite happy with my job, however being a band [number removed] for the last [timeframe removed] has made me feel like we don't matter.  We as a team have been fighting for a band [number removed] for a number of years and I feel that the managers have done everything in their power to stop this.  It is appalling that this has been allowed.  It has made me feel that we don't matter.
I am struggling with work at the minute not enough staff to do the role.
I am thinking of retire and return.  The last medical secretary was turned down to do this and has since left the Trust.  I will ask and see what the outcome is before making any decisions.  Thanks.
I am very grateful to my colleagues, managers and other management of my organisation.  And I love my patients, most/all most all of them are wonderful.
I am very satisfied with my working life in my team within Merseycare.
I applied to reduce my hours , I have had to wait for over many months for the organisation to recruit another member of staff to allow me to finally reduce my working week from 4 days to 3.
I believe that Mersey Care should be trying and helping progress it's own employees more and reward them with more opportunity for those who wish to move up the ladder and progress professionally.

I believe the main problem we face at the moment is with high sickness absences.  This is monitored by line managers within the team accordingly but rarely any action taken once staff reach stage 3, this seems to be due to to delays/push back from HR.  I believe this is causing a lot of wasted resource, money and time and also causes a decrease of staff morale constantly having to cover sickness.

I come under admin staff and feel that we are expected to do to much for our current job role i.e. expected to cover a wide range of duties including your own job in different venues - which at the moment is impossible, due to lack of staff.  This makes me feel indispensable.  I feel that where health and safety for staff comes is always down to costing.  Staff should feel safe in their workplace and I do not at present.

I currently work at City Centre district nursing.  I love my role and I an happy working in this area.  Th only thing that puts me off working in the area is the parking fines.  I would consider leaving this team should we not get any support regards to parking tickets.  When visiting a patient in the city centre I am constantly rushing as I am worried about where I have parked which is not good for the patient.

I do feel valued and appreciated by my colleagues within my team however, we are not supported by operational and clinical leads at all.  The service is in crisis and we have been made to feel it is our fault.  The team as a whole feel deflated and defeated.  Some members have gone to freedom to speak.  RCN are also investigating and supporting the team going forward.  We are being managed by people who have no clue on what is going on on the ground.  And for those that do know the role they are dismissing our concerns and have an answer for everything.  Like politicians.  To run a DN service effectively and safely you need to know the role of the DN.

I do my job, because I love to do it and I enjoy making a difference in people's life.  But the way that the health has been approached is fragmented and inefficient.  I would recommend to offer different opportunities to the patients, where in one place all the needs are sorted while the patient is visiting.  Also, trusts' education programs should promote holistic perspective to diagnose and treat, and when possible to provide with more physical contact and appreciation than given self-care responsibilities, to people that are living lives with isolation and minimal common sense..  Currently, the opportunities to see and talk with a person to receive help, are very limited and humans need humans to find the way throw their difficulties.

I don't feel the organisation listen to our team as a whole.  I feel they hear what they want to hear and don't resolve issues.  For example, since COVID we have been moved base 4 or maybe 5 times.  Number of desks gets smaller each time.  We are now in an office that is out of area to our patients so travel time to base we could have had clinical contact instead with patients.  As a band 5 I feel I would benefit from an office base that everyone feels they can travel to within work hours so that I have support to ask questions rather than over the phone.  Physio is a hands on job and I need hands on guidance not remote.  Also, I feel the office issue has affected our teams health and wellbeing.  We work remotely within community and it is difficult to set boundaries and we often work over our hours and through are lunch breaks.  The team are absolutely amazing and I feel we are not appreciated with the way we are treated, recently another change to our desk space without any consultation.  I feel our operational lead has a difficult job and is not very present because they are too busy with higher management issues.  All this leads to is poor staff retention and lack of job satisfaction and team with low morale and people will end up burning out.

I don't know how these surveys reach useful conclusions.  NHS needs to address ''most senior person knows best'' culture.  Insightful decisions are not made by people who have made themselves well informed about services.  People do not understand or value the roles of others.  Different teams could work much better together; hostility towards other teams is common.  Surveys ask about the manager immediately above, but the impact upon wellbeing and effectiveness in role is often influenced by other managers.
I don't like the pay step in the bandings.  It infers that we haven't learnt a thing in a year and our experience not valued.  We should have our increments yearly for all grades.

I don't think we get the support from management they just talk down to us never support there staff.  We need new computers in the team to do our job as staff waiting round for staff to come of computers.  Home visits there is no support we keep telling management that there is areas that we need parking permit and they don't listen so then some staff have had parking fine due to parking at pts house.  We have a clinic in Liverpool rope walk and due to parking staff get in to work an hour early to get a parking bay.

I enjoy all aspects of my work apart from unnecessary paperwork which sometimes is felt like it is done for the sake of doing it.  The incident reporting system and processes are simplified and streamlined, it would make clinician's job much more efficient and result in effective service delivery.

I enjoy my job role as a health care assistant within[location removed].  My team leader is extremely supportive, encouraging approachable.  I feel my role [details removed] is rewarding fulfilling and you realise end of life matters.
I enjoy working for Mersey Care.
I enjoy working for the trust and feel they offer plenty of opportunity to improve yourself and progress.

I enjoy working with my current team, we all have a respectful strong relationship and support each other.  I also feel very supported by my team leader with regular catch up.  I do not feel supported by higher management, My self and my team lead feel we have no autonomy or say with regards to our own team.  Regularly feel disregarded and ignored by higher management who have no interest in our or our staffs mental health wellbeing.  Higher management have "regular meetings" which feel nothing more than just a tick box exercise with little or no difference seen.  8-8 where brought in without considering staff needs, again this felt like a tick box exercise, the reasons for the 8-8 has not helped and instead stretched services, no continuity in patient care and services are now short which has a knock on effect within the teams as we are constantly helping and picking up other calls for teams.  I left a team where I wanted to remain due to the constant stress I was under.  I started a new post which felt critically unsafe daily with no support from senior leadership with hurtful comments made.  I was currently having MSK issues which required surgery to fix this, and I think if my operation date didn't come when it did I probably would have ended up off sick with anxiety and stress.

I enjoy working within my team, I feel appreciated most of the time and have no concerns with the direct team.  At times senior management make changes that seem impractical and will not listen to the concerns of the staff, at least initially.

I feel admin should be tiered and paid according to the level you are on.  Not all admin do the same work or carry the same responsibilities, yet we are all paid a Band 3 salary.  Our admin team, for example, could be a Band 3 but at a higher tier salary than another admin team because of the nature of our work.  I feel we are underpaid for the responsibilities we carry.

I feel admin staff are treated differently than clinical staff in the NHS, for example flexible working and working from home!  Clinicians nursery nurses school nurses can all work from home, but admin in my team are not allowed and the explanation being other members of staff have been abusing it?  The times I have been allowed to work from home all my work gets done, so why not tackle the problem with the individuals who are abusing it?  I feel that's a big problem in the NHS and I have worked here for over [timeframe removed].
I feel appreciated by the clinical team that I work for, but feel that generally administration is undervalued and is often overlooked as a service group in the NHS.
I feel as if we are not valued an respected the moral with staff members is low our team leaders and Band 5 are approachable it is the management on a higher level.
I feel as though there is a strong team identity in my Trust.  However, often, it becomes challenging where the NHS is attempting to collaborate with other organisations - e.g.  The city council.
I feel communication within the service could be improved to reduce anxieties and a clear plan as to where the service is going.
I feel completely undervalued and over stretched at work.  We have managers who are more concerned about kpi's than staff welfare.

I feel extremely let down by my SLT, after a period of sickness I returned to work and my role had changed.  Not one of my SLT kept me updated during my sick leave or upon my return that my job role had changed.  I have been criticised by my line manager and offered little support since my return.  I feel undervalued/ignored/demotivated and hurt since my return.  Supervision is a tick box exercise with little value other than to support the organisations target and manager that 'issues' are documented with no follow up or resolution.
I feel fully supported within my team.

I feel like a valued member of my team.  I feel confident that I could raise any concerns with them and they would be managed accordingly.  My team is friendly and approachable and I would 100% recommend the team.  As a team, we have high standards and go above and beyond for our clients.

I feel like immediate members of staff in my team (e.g. team leaders and other clinicians) are doing everything we can to support one another.  The work load is getting heavier with more procedures/admin tasks to be done (radars/reporting), chasing things up with other organisations and patients becoming more complex.  We cannot recruit anyone, this is having an affect on us all.  Moods are low and people are fed up at times.
I feel like the joy of working is being slowly stripped away through micromanagement and a distinct lack of comprehension/respect for roles.

I feel Mersey care could be improved by having more nurses on the floor, there are to many managers who cannot do the role they are appointed into, less band 8 roles and more clinical staff throughout the trust, especially in the community.

I feel Mersey Care has caused a lot of staff to leave because of poor management.  Bullying tactics.  I have never known staff morale to be so low.  Nurses can't use their own ability and skill to make decisions there is a tick box for everything it's too much.  Its becoming a dictatorship not a workplace.  Been a nurse for [number removed] years never felt like this.

I feel most of the time not supported enough by my managers in band 8 and above, we do not have face to face meeting even though we have safety huddle we do not get our chance to speak about our issues, and also even when we speak about the problems they will not get sorted.
I feel my division cares more about performance then patient safety.  Often feel decisions at an SLT level are very short sighted.  Unreasonable work load pressure at times.

I feel my immediate manager is very supportive, they listens to my concerns and will always but my health before work.  I do struggle sometimes with the higher managers and the way they communicate things with me especially when changes need to be made in the way I work.
I feel our role is not appreciated and despite staffing pressures within the team staff are often moved at short notice to support other service lines.  This is very unsettling for the team.
I feel over worked feel under appreciated under valued and think the pay in disgraceful.
I feel really fortunate to work the manager that I do, they are very supportive, understanding and an all round great person.

I feel supported by my line manager and my team but not the higher management who have recently brought about an organisational change and left us in chaos.  One [job title removed] changed our off duty and didn't reply to several emails.

I feel supported by our managers and higher up managers too, I feel that I am able to approach managers and colleagues to discuss work when required.  I feel that the managers also encourage us to develop our careers further.

I feel that all the correct things are said however sometimes this feels like management speeches, not personal or individual.  The lack of staff and lack of vacancies other than mental health roles does not make any improvement to staff moral.  Staff are exhausted but keep being told that the ratios are correct.  It feels like there is an ever increasing gap between those working on the frontline and senior leaders.  The rules are so conflictive where staff have to wear uniform in the office environment however senior leadership can wear what they like?  It feels very divided and lip service is paid to the struggling teams out there.
I feel that although we voice that we are over loaded they don't listen and we have voiced this so many times.

I feel that as a district nurse working x5 days a week I often feel I do not have a good work life balance however I feel with our current salary I would be unable to drop my hours to part time.  I am also aware that in the field I work in within my organisation I am not able to do long days as they are not offered.  I also feel that I cannot do bank work as this would affect my mental health as I already work full time hours.  I also feel that I have had stressful times over the past year due to car breakdowns.  As my work requires me to use my own car to visit patients this has a detrimental effect on the service and makes me feel guilty if I am without a car due to breakdown.  I also feel we do not get enough money in terms of expenses for wear and tear on our own vehicles being used daily to sometimes travel up to 30 miles a day.
I feel that as admin we are overlooked with being able to work from home.

I feel that at times staff behaviours are not dealt with, there is an unfair disparity between staff with and those without disabilities.  Staff with children are not always supported.  Communication is not always the best and the organisation feels at times like a dictatorship/done to rather than with organisation.

I feel that changing the hours within the District Nursing service has stretched the day staff leaving us short staffed with the same workload.  The 5-8 shift, there are more staff available with often less call outs.  Continuity of care is difficult to achieve as staff are expected to pick up work from other teams as they are now short staffed in the day.  Morale is at an all time low within the teams.  Sickness within the teams is increasing.  Its just so sad that an amazing well lead District Nursing service is now struggling.
I feel that management (*including wider management) are more focussed on targets and outcomes rather than wellbeing of staff and patients.
I feel that our team has an excellent care culture - very nurturing and a lovely team to work in.

I feel that some KPIs are unachievable, unfair and take away from patient care, for example access rates.  I also feel the new 15% annual leave KPI affects having a healthy work life balance when annual leave is not granted.

I feel that the staffer staffing model needs to be implemented appropriately - to avoid staff burn out, stress and pressures.  Particularly with winter pressures impending.  We as a team work on minimum staffing number meaning staff sickness etc. really does impact service delivery - leading to long waits and stressful working environments.

I feel that the Trust's Respect and Civility Policy favours the perpetrator and doesn't consider the feelings of the person on the receiving end, e.g. of bullying in the workplace.  I don't feel mediation is always the answer and the Trust should take appropriate disciplinary action in some cases.

I feel that there could be more support for staff with neuro divergent conditions especially around recruitment and selection process.  I do not feel that staff are aware of what adjustments can be requested for interview and there is not anything to request on the interview acceptance form as it is more related to physical limitations.  I do feel I was disadvantaged at a recent interview due to this as there was an unseen presentation which I could not fully complete due to my protected characteristics.  I believe that the service line in which I work has struggled as it has only recently had quality nursing posts added to SLT and an addition of an AHP lead would contribute to the team immensely.  Although the addition of Microsoft teams meetings have been a gamechanger and improved communication it also means that sometimes meetings can be back to back throughout the day with little chance to catch up on actions adding to workload pressures.  Throughout the division there seems to be some evidence that people are promoted into senior positions that do not have the necessary experience or knowledge for the roles they have taken on, this has led to a lack of robust leadership and decision making that impacts on teams.  Although I do enjoy my job and will always give 100% I also feel that the last 12 months have been difficult with sickness and vacancies impacting on my abilities to fulfil my role to the best of my abilities as I have been picking up work for absent colleagues.  I also feel that my contribution to the service line was not understood or acknowledged and as a result I feel there is nowhere further that I can progress my career and if anything has had a severe impact on my confidence.  I want to acknowledge that my colleagues in my team and the clinical teams are amazing and work tirelessly to ensure that the patients get the best experience from them and they flex on a regular basis if new pathways are introduced or piloted to ensure that patient can receive the right treatment in their own home.

I feel that there needs to be more equality in the management structures in the trust, there are staff line managing larger teams of 30+ staff while others managers within the same division are managing small teams of less than 10 staff.  In my role for the past 12 months at least 50% of that time I have had no operational manager in place.  This has meant that I have received very infrequent management supervision and support, been a new manager supporting over 30 staff I feel that this is not adequate making the role very difficult.

I feel that this trust is bullying staff and changes rules and regulations to suit what they want to do.  A member of staff in my team is being treated appallingly currently by senior management.  I would not recommend Mersey Care as a trust to work for.

I feel that when I first joined this organisation a few years ago; the trust was an excellent place to work.  Sadly; over recent years there has been a huge decline in the overall culture, especially from senior management.  I often see what I would call bullying behaviours on meetings and feel that recruitment to senior posts are already a; ''done deal'' prior to even been advertised, there has been a definite increase in nepotism in regards to recruitment, especially around family members of senior leaders getting jobs and being promoted.

I feel that within MERSEYCARE the goal of expansion and increasing wealth is often main priority rather then excelling at patient care.  The trust takes over vast areas of care however never reinvests back into the existing one, it loads more work onto already stretched services and then expects everyone to just concede to the new way of working despite it being potentially unsafe.  It feels as if whomever bids for the services we take on has no clue about what is actually happening on the ground and just decides to take on work passing the weight down through the management structure to the staff on the ground, expecting miracles that are unreasonable and not what staff actually want.  The expectation to work within this archaic structure of 'I say and you do' is soo antiquated.  The staff are highly intelligent, flexible and adaptive individuals with good insight and excellent care but this is stretched thin when the trust demands raise and the capacity does not reflect.  WE are not transparent to our partners in PCNs or ICBs, we hide the fact that staffing isn't safe or within line to receive the volume of work we have agreed to and we don't do anything well.  WE scrape by doing things to ensure the homeostasis however we don't do anything very well or excel at anything.

I feel that within the organisation, our work load is continuing to increase and we are expected to just get on with it.  On days when our team are struggling, I feel the problems are papered over and no real plan is put in place for a long term goal.  More and more is expected of us, but when things go wrong, I still feel we have a blame culture and are not supported.  We struggle through more and more work loads, and there is no cut off, we are expected to just continue, sometimes with no support at all.
I feel the main priority for Mersey care is mental health care/workers rather than the adult nursing areas.

I feel the NHS management culture is to listen with closed ears.  It is all lip service with no action following any discussions.  I quite often give suggestions on how to improve the service, this is always received with a closed book but then later on (maybe a few months later), my suggestion is put in force.  It is as if they don't like non-management making suggestions.  There is definitely a pecking order culture, even though in my former employment I was had a high profile managerial position!

I feel the organisation is very focused on saving money to the detriment of patient care.  There is no succession planning, feel that the organisation on a whole are not proactive for planning for the future and ensuring teams are safely staffed prior to staff leaving.  Budgets are not regularly reviewed and haven't been updated for 10 + years to reflect the patient complexity and acuity.
I feel the senior leadership team need to work on empathic communication and understanding roles of those who work in teams.  Too much of a gap between expectations and understanding of roles.
I feel there should be a better system/strategy to show recognition for others.

I feel there should be recognition for length of service.  Whilst immediate managers are supportive and show appreciation of experience, the trust should be able to award something to recognise those who have significant experience at the top of their bands, often performing above their bands due to their experience.  The trust needs to explore further opportunities for clinical specialist posts as currently you can only progress as an AHP past a B7 if you go into management/ non patient facing job role.

I feel this survey is generic and does not represent the percentage of the workforce that work in Merseycare.  It is a liberal tick box exercise that does not meet the work force population within Merseycare, maybe allow additional comments in each section to get true feedback and not the percentages outcome that you will get from a tick box exercise.

I feel this trust as a culture of you do as I say and are not approachable to staff, they don't listen to staff concerns nor try to resolve this.  Going to freedom of speak doesn't help either as we as a team have approach this route with no resolution for [timeframe removed] and we are still waiting.
I feel undervalued and by the organisation (not my immediate manager).  Staff are not always listened too.  Sometimes staff are given lip service.

I feel upper management is lead too much by KPIs and productivity relating to number of patient visits rather than quality of visits.  This is not a true reflection of the work load we do on a daily basis which is complex, qualitative, and multi-faceted.
I feel valued by my team but not the organisation.

I feel valued within my team, we are actively encouraged to have daily catch up meetings and informal supervision, discuss any concerns or problems.  My concerns are with senior members of the board who look into Emis diaries regarding (quantity)how many visits?  Rather than quality.

I feel very disillusioned with the Trust at present, it feels too big with not enough corporate support to support services.  Estates issues are not always addressed in a timely manner.  HR support has improved since NWB joined although you can wait for days for advice which makes it difficult to manage staff or situations.  Policies do not match with some of the new systems we are using, too many systems in the management of staff.  Lack of communication from Commissioner's and contract updates are non existent and we are expected to deliver the same services on a contract that was awarded 10 years ago which is impossible within the current budget and the demand for services has increased dramatically with the patients becoming increasingly complex.

I feel very settled and happy within my team.  My manager is very supportive and approachable, which keeps the staff moral high.  My colleagues are great to work with, and we are very supportive to each other.  I feel lucky to be part of this amazing team!
I feel very supported by my team and organisation, I feel this is due to the culture of my team and good management.
I feel very supported in my work place by my colleagues and especially by my manager.
I feel well supported by my team interim manager and also by their ops manager.

I found that when some colleagues have been given the opportunity to step into a role for months, with the belief they will be accepted to complete the role and stay in the role if proven to be good at that job role for years to come.  But then not be informed they aren't allowed to continue in that role and they have been informed they no longer have a job role within that team, when their is known vacancies for that job role they have been doing.  Then they have to wait to hear if they have a job to go to, when other staff have been brought in their place and they then have no job and they end up leaving for another job role out of this trust which is a huge loss of expertise and fantastic skilled nurses.

I generally enjoy my job and value my work.  The most challenging part of work is targets which are often difficult to meet due to the nature in which information is collated.  For example, a client who drops out of treatment following 2 or 3 sessions impacts on therapists overall recovery rate.  I have also noticed an increase in more challenging and complex clients over the past 12 months without any changes to caseload numbers or having extra time to treat this client group.  I would recommend my role to others however feel with a few changes it could be an even better place to work.
I generally enjoy my job, however some colleague's behaviours are very unprofessional and I do wonder if this is tackled my higher management.

I get annoyed with being pressurised by my organisation to fill in this questionnaire every time it is produced.  Just so they can get their % up!  I see little point in completing it.  No changes are produced as a result of it.  No feedback is received.  It feels a waste of time.  And why does it matter if my sex is the same as my birth sex.  There are 2 genders male and female.  The correct grammar is he or she.  So why all these ridiculous questions?  All the NHS management side cares about is ticking boxes, passing an inspection etc.  Nobody shows much interest in the day to day issue of staff numbers between these inspections.  Report unsafe staffing - we do!  Nothing changes.  Its been like this for years.  I have worked in the NHS for [timeframe removed].

I had to raise formal concerns about the service and colleagues clinical work around 12 months ago.  An action plan was written but I never had an outcome to this despite chasing it up.  There are ongoing issues with colleagues talking about each other, this is an issue from clinical leads down and has never in any way been successfully addressed.  I am [condition removed] and have unfortunately had a lot of [condition removed] related sickness.  My experience is there is little support or understanding of how [condition removed] related sickness should be treated or that somebody may not have a straight forward [condition removed] - I have had attempts to trigger maternity leave and told I would at face disciplinary for further [condition removed]  related sickness.  Clear irritation if I haven't been well enough to work and on my return senior staff won't ask how I am but will email about picking up more work etc.  I really feel that management would benefit from some training around how to support pregnant staff and particularly those who are experiencing [condition and details removed].  I had to ask repeatedly for [details removed] risk assessment.  Basic things like return to works (not even a phone discussion to see how I am) and updating my [details removed] risk assessment haven't been done.
I have a fantastic and very supportive team and I feel extremely supported within my role.
I have a good working relationship with my line manager.

I have a lot of family problems at the moment and feel that my managers have been very understanding of these problems and have let me take family members to appointments as and when needed and have let me make this time up as and when I am able to.  I appreciate this a lot.

I have absolute faith in my manager.  However, the Trust as a whole is money orientated and the feel is that they really don't care about staff.  I'd have my team look after my family but not the organisation.  We don't get breaks so there's an hour that I don't get paid for and because of that I'm working for an hour every shift.  There is no organisation between MDT's.  Trying to get hold of someone outside of our team is impossible and this impacts patients.  Moral is horrendous.  I'm not sure what higher management are getting paid to do, which is handsome, and what they're actually doing.  I don't think people outside of each team realise how others are struggling.  We are angry fed up and working for free.  Things are at breaking point.
I have always enjoyed my job and love working in a team.  I feel important in my job role and my colleagues are really happy with my work too.

I have been in my job in community Phlebotomy for many years under North West Boroughs.  Since we were taken over by Merseycare the job is not what it was, we are on minimum wage and considering we are one of the busiest services we are being put under pressure on a daily basis, I consider myself as a hard worker but this is stressing me out, I've tried to explain mistakes will happen if we are put under more pressure.  Knowsley Phlebotomy do a great Job but I'm so thinking of walking away.  Nobody listens to us and it is so frustrating.  Someone in this organisation needs to listen to our concerns instead of just expecting us to do as we are told, Feeling bullied to be honest and as an adult this does not feel great.

I have been in this current role since [date removed] so some of the answers related to my previous position so is this looking at the full overall score as my role now!  [timeframe removed] community nursing.  [date removed] - present bladder and bowel role.

I have been working in Mersey Care for a few years now and it is a trust I am proud to work for.  Compared to the previous trust I worked for, the senior leadership is very visual and supports all staff.  I will never leave Mersey Care as I feel valued, and would encourage people to work for this organisation.
I have completed this survey out of work time.

I have completed this survey reflecting on my department as I feel valued by the Dental directorate but not with Mersey Care.  I have had to use my own annual leave to see my daughter through cancer and chemo treatment which I think is disgusting.  I applied myself for the long service award and I'm still waiting 12 months on!

I have felt very pressured and stressed within this organisation, I would actively encourage friends or family not to work for this organisation, listening to senior management stating "its a service requirement".

I have flexible working now, but this was difficult to get but now it is in place it works well.  Sometimes get one bad person who can spoil a team, and this is not addressed.  I have allocated work and meet my targets, but I know others in the tam do not as we have a weekly meeting and things are shred.  To me this should be private as they might feel bad, and I feel I'm doing more than others.  Best thing is my team and enjoy spending time in work due to them, too much working from home takes this away.
I have found working for Merseycare frustrating at times due to the lack of communication, systems get change i. e. Oh referrals and you are not informed you have to find out for yourself.

I have great concerns regarding the lack of staff in Community dental services, lack of recruitment to replace staff leading to more pressure being put on current staff causing multiple mistakes and near misses.

I have had [number removed] incident and [number removed] very serious operation in the [timeframe removed] I've been working in the phlebotomist department and in these occasions I feel I was not supported by this organisation what so ever, I find we are not supported in any way.

I have no faith that this organisation with take care of the physical and mental health needs of staff when it comes to managing case loads.  This has historically resulted in staff leaving and going off sick.  The manager is extremely supportive of people attending courses to progress in their career and for me this outweighs my current thoughts to leave.  Admittedly I love my role, the families I work with, and my team.  If it wasn't for them and access to training courses I feel I would have considered leaving more seriously that I currently do.
I have really enjoyed working in Mersey Care, and all the support from my team and the trust has been amazing, and has allowed me to thrive within my role.

I have recently changed teams.  My new team is better managed and I feel supported.  My previous team had several toxic nurses who were unprofessional and nothing was dealt with despite numerous complaints.  I do not feel Mersey care take concerns from staff seriously.

I have recently moved to my current department from a different department within this organisation and have worked for this organisation for just over a year.  My experience of some of the topics in the survey therefore are limited but what I have experienced up to now has mostly been positive.

I have seen there has been a drive for the recruitment of staff and this will, in time, help relieve some of the pressure felt by the staff on the shop floor.  My concern is the retention of keeping the experience staff that are already employed by the Trust.  Since staring within the UTCs/WICs I have spoken to many staff across the different sites and there is an undercurrent of unhappiness in the role, the staffing and the pressures faced daily, with many looking for new roles.  This feeds into my comment around not feeling valued as employees as it does not feel like wellbeing is being supported as expected in the Trust strategy.  Recruitment is no quick or easy task but I feel it is important to understand the effect this is having on the staff on the shop floor, feeling unmotivated, tired and under valued.

I have worked for NHS for many years, but several years ago I was down banded which caused me great anxiety and stress leaving me feeling undervalued.  I did contemplate leaving the NHS but as I was of a certain age and enjoyed my role within the service I decided to stay until retirement.

I have worked for the NHS for many years and had minimal sick leave.  I have had one day sick leave for a migraine within 4 years (including working through COVID on the wards).  I feel that the Merseycare sickness policy is awful.  I had to ring a number and justify why I was off sick, then wait for a return call from higher management that prevented me from resting.  Further to this I had text messages and constant reminders as to when I would be returning (from the sickness team).  I think this is harassment and unjustified.  If I am off with a migraine then I should be left alone to sleep.  I returned to work the following day not feeling great.  This is not a reflection on my immediate managers but on the Mersey Care sickness policy.

I have worked for the NHS for many years.  I have had various roles over the years but now I work as a Receptionist for Knowsley Clinical Admin Team.  I have never seen so many staff who are so unhappy, feeling so unappreciated and have no flexibility or say towards their rota and ways of working.  Staff are constantly giving ideas to management to make our team run more efficiently and effectively for the service needs and for staff to have a healthy work/life balance but unfortunately management just say "No".  No explanation given, just "NO".  Management style is micromanagement, Bullying people, shouting at staff.  I have never seen this before or worked in these conditions before.  I look forward to retirement.

I have worked for this organisation for many years and in this time I have seen a deterioration in the way that staff are treated.  Unfortunately work related stress has increased to record highs and this needs looking into.  The high turnover of staff, due to staff leaving the trust is unprecedented and this is becoming an unsafe work environment for staff and patients alike due to staffing issues and unrealistic workloads.

I have worked for this organisation since qualifying many years ago and have been supported throughout my career to return to university on three occasions to continuously further my professional and academic knowledge, all whilst bringing up a young family.  I have extremely understanding and supportive managers who will always strive to do the best for us.

I have worked in the same department for the last [timeframe removed].  I do so because I have a fantastic direct line manager.  They put their staff first yet ensures our department maintains the highest standards.  I would be unlikely to stay in the Trust if they were to leave as I feel they give our department its identity and direction.

I have worked under different senior leadership teams within the organisation and I would like to say that some are far superior in terms of displaying of trust values and the culture they promote and how they interact and listen to their staff.

I have, in the main, enjoyed working for the NHS.  I do think that more should be done within the organisation to deal properly with any members of staff who either cause others stress, bully people, speak in a disrespectful way, or don't pull their weight.  These people are in the minority, but seem to carry on regardless.
I hope this is anonymous.

I just hate my job now after[timeframe removed] of working for the NHS as a nurse.  My current Trust is the worst I have ever worked for they do not care about the staff at all do not listen which obviously impacts the patients in our care.
I love Mersey Care!

I love my job and enjoy the variety of tasks I undertake.  The most important aspect and positive impact for me has been working from home for most of my week; not having to commute on public transport (I don't drive) has freed up so much of my leisure time.  The only time I feel under stress is when my colleague is on annual leave and I'm having to try to keep on top of the workload on my own.

I love my job and find it challenging and rewarding every day.  I have excellent managers and work colleagues, working for a Respiratory Team that provides excellent care and for which I am proud to work with.

I love my job in terms of managing and supporting my staff members.  They are my priority and I have a responsibility to ensure they are happy, psychologically safe and have the suitable resources for their individual needs to assist them in completing their duties.

I much prefer the morals and values of my position in the NHS compared to my previous private employment.  I am not a band 2 however our band 2 admins work with duties befitting band 3 as regular day-to-day activities if someone goes above and beyond their job description this should be reflected in usable reward, they deserve a chance to progress and fight for their right to appropriate pay.  One banding system does not work across all departments, they are not all a booking service.  We hear about clinical patient-facing understaffing and under pay, but the administration side seem not to get the recognition.  We cannot function without them.

I often do well above my contracted hours for no extra pay due to pressures of my caseload.  I often feel stressed and mentally exhausted and this affects my personal life.  My work life balance needs to be reviewed.  It is the nature of the job and I accept it is the way I work, but I feel to do my job the way I want to - I have too many pressures.  This is the same of a lot of the other nurses in our team - that the job is undoable and can be extremely stressful.  I also want to partially retire and reduce my hours- to be able to claim my pension that I should have claimed in [date removed] as I have Special class status and can claim my pension at [age removed].  ([age and date removed]).  I don't want to just retire and return as I feel that I need to reduce my work hours to be able to improve my work/life balance.  I enjoy my role and feel well supported, I don't really want to leave my role but I want to take my pension and feel like I can't currently in my current role.  I understand that it is a full time job- My manager and Team leader have considered my request but currently not able to reduce my hours.

I really enjoy my job I feel the work we complete within out team does support families within the community to improve their health and wellbeing.  I work within a special team who are supportive and look out for one another we work with students and always make sure they are included in out team and we have an excellent team leader who is always ready to listen.  The only part of my job I do not like is the pay as with the cost of living it is not enough to live a decent life outside of work, nursing is a stressful job therefore it is important to have a life outside of work were you can relax and enjoy your time off.
I really enjoy working in Mersey Care - feel that I am valued and I am lucky to have a supportive leadership team.
I really think the sickness policy needs to be reviewed as it is a huge drain on the NHS.

I sent an email regarding starting up a men's health clinic and had nothing back, also my levels of anxiety has increased due to a suspension of my salary sacrifice due to a UC claim.  I sorted the issue out within a month and the salary sacrifice still has not been sorted out for[timeframe removed] and I feel that I am accruing debt and possibly will lose the car which I require for work.

I support my organisation, and always work towards the trust values.  They invest in staff because they want their staff to stay working with the trust so they them selves can become one of the best leading organisations.

I think Mersey Care and the NHS as a whole is a fantastic employer.  The pay could be better but that is a wider systemic issue.  I'm allowed to work flexible/condensed hours which has helped with childcare through the years which has benefited my entire family.
I think Mersey Care is a great NHS trust to work for, in particular I have a very supportive manager and really appreciate the hard work that she puts in for her team.

I think more awareness need to be done regarding ethnicity as some staff are experiencing micro aggression in place of work and being discriminated at because of their accent an personality including myself.  We all contribute to the development of the organisation so there should not be discrimination in place of work regarding how anyone is perceived.

I think some of the senior management in Mersey care are happy to sweep things under the rug.  I feel like my immediate manager supports me as much as they can but I feel like those in positions above them are not supportive.  Even when we've had challenging times and begged for help they've treated us like we don't matter.  It sometimes makes it hard to respect them.
I think the Freedom to Speak is essential for me feeling comfortable in my role and how much the organisation values it makes me feel confident and supported.  Thank you for this being in place.

I think the organisation is a great place to work.  However I think the lack of communication between concurrent running projects leads to duplication of work, or projects being worked on being irrelevant.  Regarding patient care I do think it is of high priority, but not highest, I feel like seeing patients within KPI is the number one priority.  Yes this absolutely is key to ensuring timely and efficient care across large cohorts of patients, but it would be disingenuous to say that the quality of care or staff wellbeing was the number one priority.

I think the Trust should do more to help with staff Psychological Wellbeing and Emotional Resilience due to having to deal with the general public and their traumas which they offload onto us.  Also I think the Trust's needs to do a LOT more work on Population Health and Prevention instead of fire fighting by frontline staff who are only getting burnt out.  Finally, with the rising level of anxiety and concern about Climate breakdown, 3/4 of the population have expressed feeling very worried about climate change and the direction humanity is headed, I would like to bring to your attention the effect this can have on us as Mersey Care staff and frontline healthcare professionals.  It is also on the rise in later life and within our hyper-individualistic, consumer-capitalist society, it can leave a lot of us feeling isolated, alienated, and helpless.  I have not seen any support (maybe I haven't looked hard enough) on the Mersey Care website, YourBrief emails or Magazine about the topic and was wondering if anyone in you are looking into this?  Earlier this year I attended a "Climate Cafe" / Listening Circle run by a member of The Climate Psychology Alliance (CPA).  They have registered and trained practitioners who have been holding safe spaces for people carrying the burden of being climate conscious, where it is often the only outlet people have to meet with likeminded individuals and hold a safe space for exploring how "honouring our emotional responses can create strength in a time of climate and ecological breakdown".
