I met with the Psychologist afterwards who was more than happy to train others to be able to facilitate such safe spaces as they spend a lot of their time travelling between Liverpool and Manchester to deliver them.  They are beyond retirement age but continue to practice and volunteer for this cause but acknowledges they won't be able to continue for much longer and is keen to enable others to take up the baton to help with this work.  I'm sure if you did a survey of Mersey Care staff there would be quite a lot of people who are on the scale of "somewhat" to "very worried" as reported in the ONS study above.  I was wondering if this is something the Wellbeing Hub would be able to support?  I did email them this earlier in the year but didn't get any response.  Many thanks.

I think there is more opportunity for flexible working for administrative staff.  Majority of other organisations allow this but it is not widely accepted in ours.  Some managers will approve this and understand, while others don't.  I have children at home and current caring responsibilities for [family member removed], I could easily work from home and provide same standard of work, (if not more work because you don't have office interruptions) as I do in office rather than take the day off for carers leave or unpaid if I had an emergency where I couldn't come into office.  Nearly all of the nurses I work with work from home on a regular basis and some of them have small children etc too and it is never questioned and they provide clinical, patient facing work.  I think this needs reviewing to truly make flexible working more accessible.

I think this organisation is too focused on paperwork and emails, constant changes to things that work well then fail to do so when removed.  Staff moral is low, no flexibility for staff to stay, if you don't fit the rules they won't alter them to encourage staff to stay.  Management has lost interest in staff as individuals and you are just a number.  Too many management and none clinical managers who don't understand what it is like to work on the floor and cannot pitch in to help when the staffing is poor.  Not enough focus on patients rather than paperwork.  Poor communication, website isn't clear enough to allow patients to make a clear decision what we offer at each Walk in site.  Poor sickness policies where long term illnesses are staged the same as fit people, many staff leaving constantly due to the pressures.
I used to really love my job, working with children and families, but at present I feel its lots of documentation and lots of referrals.
I used to work for another trust and would never go back.  This has been the best trust to work for.  Super proud to represent Mersey Care.
I wish that senior and operational managers were more active with staff worries/concerns re bullying at work from direct team leaders or other colleagues.
I wish the trust will take the pay scale seriously so staff can afford a better living condition as its really a struggle with cost of living at the moment as NHS Staff.
I wish to take MARS due to my age and my health is not that good.
I work for the ICRAS team, Urgent Care and I have always felt valued in my role, and always feels supported by my managers.
I work with an excellent team, who are supportive of each other and the patients we see.  There is always an opportunity for formal/informal supervision.
I work within a hard working and committed team who are supportive and we can all be open and honest with each other, personal concerns stay confidential within our team.

I work within the walk in centres.  Staffing is very poor.  As a band 7 nurse I very rarely get a break due to poor staffing.  Very busy departments.  Feel like management don't listen to our concerns.  We are budgeted for a certain amount of staff yet never have that number of staff on duty.  We are struggling and not looking forward to the upcoming winter pressures.
I worked for the trust for over 10 years then left and have returned recently I think Merseycare supports staff well.
I worked in my job for many years and never felt as low as this, Merseycare are changing things and not for the best.

I would just like to say how disappointed I am with my higher admin management who are completely unsupportive.  I just feel really worthless and like I am just a number.  Colleagues in the team are leaving because of it so we are losing highly skilled brilliant staff who have been here a long time.  My immediate line manager is one of the best I have ever had and just the most lovely and caring person.  I just wish the 2 higher managers could be as amazing as her.
I would like there to be more clinical (rather than managerial) band 7 roles available within health visiting/children's services.

I would like to see the AFC review the Band 2 role of the DN Clerk/Admin within the Trust as this is role is paying £11.44 per hour which is the same as the minimum wage, this banding does not reflect the role in which as admin/ clerks work within the Trust and should be reviewed by both the Unions and the Trust Management.
I would like to see the patients admitted to us to be actually physically well enough to go through rehabilitation.  I also think safe staffing numbers need to be addressed.
I would like to work from home at all times.  I would also like to do long days in order to get work life balance for myself and because of my disability.  If I could afford to I would retire.
I would still like the flexibility of WFH.
I'm currently moved from a acute ward to a fantastic physical health ward in [number removed] months where I now feel valued and supported and a great environment and I am now enjoying going to work.
I'm not sure who my line manager is.

I've been working for my service for the last [timeframe removed].  The strains to the service have become more and more visible/significant during this time.  Treatment waiting lists have grown significantly.  Staffing levels have dropped.  Treatments have become more complex due to waiting times.  Time constraints per patient have not altered over the years I have worked for the NHS, meaning I am currently working harder per patient appointment, now than I ever have done before.  I also have the burden of patient dissatisfaction due to waiting times.  The days can become tiresome with constant complaints and maxing out efforts at work constantly.  I don't know the answer to the above problems but I can not see myself continuing within the NHS as I get towards retirement, I don't believe I would be physically capable to do this job full time into retirement.

I've had experience of working here and also as a patient receiving.  Overall for the most part as an employee it is quiet good and run smoothly.  However there are definitely improvements that need to be made in terms of supporting your employees.  Last year work kindly paid for a [condition removed] and it got confirmed that I do [condition removed], following this I was made aware by my manager I can apply for access to work to help with reasonable adjustments that have been recommended by the DWP.  [number removed] months later have passed and I have only received [details removed] recommended in my report which is disappointing.  The [number removed] that I have now only just received was delayed because IT were refusing to install it because of security measures or something from what I have been told despite my manager being told that she can go ahead and order the programmes.  Anyway the issue was resolved after a few months but I am still without some recommendations and I am fairly new into my diagnosis so I struggle to explain to people how my [condition removed] affects me and believe that I should at least be given a chance to try and access the recommendations suggested.  My manager is supportive and has been throughout the whole process but I just cant believe things take this long for certain decisions to be made.  I actually feel sorry for any future employees who go onto complete the process.  IT sayings there's things in place for [condition removed] like what tell me give me links to things be clear with instructions as advice.  My experience as a patient within Talking therapies has also been a journey.  Having waited over a year to access the therapy again I was told I was receiving my therapy through an extended service who now has a contract with the NHS to deliver therapy's to Mersey Care patients.  I was told only [number removed] sessions to which I couldn't believe (having previously had therapy from talk for around 3 months) you only build a relationship with the therapist after [number removed] weeks never mind getting [number removed] weeks of sessions in all honestly how can that be effective for anyone trying to help themselves learn new skills and coping mechanisms to help them cope with their mental health.  After my feedback I give I was then offered the full [number removed] sessions that I was told I should have.  I think an overview of a how the trust helps and supports the patients in talking therapies could do with a review and how certain triage decisions are made.  Its clear from my experience as a patient how much statistics are taken into account rather than patient care ( with the questionnaires that are sent out before each appointment) and to be fair also in my role I notice how the statistics are used.  It only makes patients feel like they are a number and that their care is not at the forefront and aim of the care they should be receiving.  There is so much good with how Mersey Care is ran in terms of being an employee and the benefits and security you can feel from it (e.g. sick pay, reasonable time for appts and flexible working) but there is also so much more they could do to help both employees and patients.

ICB and commissioning services is creating service gaps the patients are falling through and make it difficult to holistically care for patients and exacerbates long term health problems, mental health concerns and staff stress.

If you raise a worry no matter how you do it you just keep going round and round until you just give up.  Issues take ages to even be looked at.  A lot is expected from people even when there are other things that need to be done.

Improvements needed to IT - computers that work, retain charge and internet access remotely,.  Improvements needed to HR and recruitment.  Need to respond to teams where demand is greater than capacity and investment in recruitment is needed.  Many contracts with previous CCGs ( now ICBs) are historic and no longer recognise the increase in demand and patient complexity.

In my role we are expected to do things that cause back ache i.e. bandaging limbs of very obese patients and having to take the weight/load of each leg.  This causes pain to back /neck.  Also we don't have access to e roster and we are only only given 3 weeks off duty in advance.  This goes against the advice given by NHS England.  It is very difficult to organise social life.
In relation to leadership, our direct team lead is very good the issues we encounter are above.

In terms of mileage claim, staff like myself and others find it so difficult to input the mileages to make claims hence leaving myself and others out without being refunded with amounts of mileages driven for.  I feel this should automatically be carried out by a special staff for this otherwise staff should be refunded with certain amounts of money that gets unclaimed.  It would be of immense relieve to see the organisation looking into these as many of us - staff feel hurt regarding this despite much efforts put into reaching patients and offering them much relieve.

In the main office we had a jigsaw that was on out of sight and on a unused worktop.  The jigsaw was done by all members of staff when they had breaks in their heavy and demanding workloads and would often dip into the jigsaw and gave us all a very small gap and relieved a bit of stress to carry on with the day.  After a recent inspection it was ordered to be removed as a member of staff was supposedly sitting on a chair doing the jigsaw and looked unprofessional.  I can categorically deny that anyone was sitting there as the worktop is too high and no one has that much time!  Considering this a mental health trust it really doesn't ask how staff are and what can help throughout the day.

In the past I have always felt included in changes proposed within my area of work however over the past 2 to 3 years this has significantly changed with little engagement from the senior leadership team in change management, I along with my team colleagues regularly comment on how we now feel "done to" rather than "done with".  This is causing a lot of unrest and anxiety to and resulting in staff looking for other jobs outside of this organisation.  It appears Mersey care are more interested in ticking boxes rather than individuals health and well being, for example on a personal level following S&A period after a very difficult [details removed] no return to work was conducted, however stage 1 of the S&A policy was invoked.  A more recent S&A episode resulted in a meeting for PDR scheduled before a return to work interview, whilst this may seem trivial to some as a previous seasoned operational manager I would say priorities appear to be somewhat questionable and may lead to feeling of the organisational priorities coming well before their employees well being.  Staff are moved to other areas without discussions leading to unrest and staff welfare only taken in to consideration once staff sides support has been requested.

Inexperienced managers are making mistakes in the management of difficult personalities.  Views of staff ignored.  No changes after awaydays.  Some staff have stopped speaking out as it makes no difference.

It is disappointing that we are now being told that wearing uniform is mandatory - when working with children and young people in the community, a uniform can often be a barrier and can lead to dis-engagement.  It is a shame that engagement is lessening due to this barrier as some children and young people will refuse to be seen in college/school if in our uniform.

It is mostly a good place to work, however due to the service being so busy, it leaves little time or support for any development/support/mentor opportunities to secure a higher position, which makes me want to move on and apply for other positions.
It is nice that my employers want to hear feedback from staff and will act on feedback received.  I am well supported in my role.
It is upsetting to be accused of 'complaining', when all you did was ask for help on a busy virtual ward.  It is unsafe practice, and I am sick of raising these issues only to be ignored.

It is very challenging at the moment as my staff are going through a consultation review, and it is very challenging, my staff our very unhappy about the new change in Job Description, and as much as I support them, the moral is very low, we do try and reassure them, but since we have merged with other services from Liverpool and Sefton, it has been a bit of a nightmare, as staff are unhappy about working across the Merseycare footprint.
It would be better if each service helps each other rather than passing the buck.

It would be nice if years of service are recognised within the NHS and celebrated to help make the staff feel more valued.  Brother in law works for the fire Brigade and they put a big emphasis on this i.e. celebration for 20 years service, 30 years service etc.
It would be nice to support and increase pay bands when a band 4 hits the top of the banding as trusts are now assessing workers on individuals experience.
It wouldn't matter what I wrote Merseycare bosses stick together.

Its been very difficult over the last couple of years trying to adapt to constant ongoing changes to our service.  We have recently increased the amount of managers we have as part of transformational change, however this is impacting with having more work and less time to adjust to changes to how we work.  This has also increased the lack of communication with the amount of layers of managers to go through before getting a response and clear guidance if at all.  Quite a lot of new colleagues to the service are inexperienced and struggle trying to become familiar and learn in their new roll.  This impacts on the staff who are trying to do business as usual with no clear guidance support or answers to concerns they have.  There also seems to be a lack of following NICE guidance, we are being told its just guidance.  I cope with this by following guidance from my professional body to adhere to code of practice.  Most staff members just want to get on with their job and not have to constantly have to change how they do this, there is a lack of feeling stable with this constant change.

Job has become too KPI orientated not being family orientated.  I have had too work with [details removed] ([condition removed]) when I am [condition removed] because I was warned I would go to the next sickness stage!  I could hardly walk or get a shoe on.  I was racially abused by a client for being [ethnicity removed] when delivering health promotion.  I reported it to my manager who did not take it seriously because "[ethnicity removed] people" cant be racially abused!  .  I have a serious [condition removed] affecting my [details removed], well known to the team.  A team building day was made for a [details removed] automatically excluding me from the team building day!
Just over a year left to work.

Just to expand on the section around pay etc, I have recently learnt there are same band roles in other teams that do not/may not have the same level of responsibility I have, this I feel is inequitable for my self and the admin team I am Team Leader for.  I would also like to elaborate that the admin team I manage are the same banding as other admin teams again with less responsibility and I don't believe the pay scales are accurate or fair and should be reviewed across all services.
Just too high expectations for number of patients to work with.

l have been health visitor for many years and I love my job.  I have always looked forward to going to work, and felt I made a difference.  However, in the past few years working to targets has really deteriorated the care and continuity that we give to our clients.  To meet these targets we often have to sacrifice good quality care.  For example, a health visitor will build up relationship with a mother over several visits then because of a 'target' further visits with that mother will be rescheduled with other staff members if the health visitor is on holiday/day off.  This can often affect the professionals/patients relationship for example, if a person was starting to feel comfortable with her health visitor she may be ready to disclose domestic violence or mental health issues but this bond can then so easily broken by a manager sending someone else to cover a non urgent visit just to meet a target.  Another example of this could be a routine birth visit which are targets say we must complete within 10 to 14 days-what is wrong with the health visitor the family now doing the birth visit on day 15 rather than somebody new having to do it within the 5 day time scale?  The midwife is still attending, it is non urgent.  Do the commissioners not want quality care?  .  My [family member removed] recently had a baby and their health visitor who they previously knew from antenatal contacts could not visit within the time scale before a weekend so they had to arrange for somebody else to visit.  The day my [family member removed] was given for this visit was the same day that they had the midwife visiting, they were attending the feeding clinic, and they were taking the baby for a hearing test.  My [family member removed] requested the visit be another day as they had so much on but the health visitor could not offer them another day and just changed their time to visit later.  I gathered that they were under pressure to visit before the weekend however doing so the visit was a complete waste of time as my [family member removed] and the baby were so tired from a busy day and they actually retained nil information from that visit.  Whereas a visit two days later would have been so much more beneficial.  I do not understand this and how this is supposed to benefit our service users.  I feel so sad that our profession is deteriorating so much and even sadder that it is affecting my own family.

Lack of engagement and involvement from managers regarding clinical staff (senior and non senior) involvement in pilots, new ways of working, development of team and service.  Staff request to be involved, requests ignored.  Feedback is not listened to or acted up on.  Lack of opportunities for staff involvement in service development, opportunity to raise concerns regarding what works well, what does not work so well, impact on patient care, and lack of action.  Staff do not feel listened to.
Lack support via senior leadership and lack of awareness of our role.  Senior management will request us to complete additional tasks with no additional time which is not realistic.
Leadership are very supportive in all aspects of work.  Team is positive and proactive.  Leadership have been VERY supportive to me personally regarding on-going social stressors at home.

Like other organisations, the trust needs to improve in areas, but they do strive to achieve this, it is important that they continue to listen to staff and service users.  The new executive will be a breath of fresh air.

Limited opportunities for career progression/to complete university courses and further enhance knowledge and skills due to funding issues and short staffing.  Trust not pro active in supporting staff development.  Staffing levels in district nursing extremely poor.  Too much money spent in mental health services which is impacting on community services.  District nurses not appreciated enough for the imperative role they play in the community caring for end of life patients and witnessing emotional events/traumas for loved ones of families.  Mental health services highly praised within the organisation and not enough praise on community services in particular district nursing.  Not enough money put into district nursing services.

Line managers are promoted far too quickly, they have limited experience of the role let alone management, they are pulled into a role then fail to communicate with the teams, the hierarchy response system means staff have to raise issues with team leaders first so managers are never seen and unsure they ever hear about issues on the shop floor for staff.  Constant firefighting, no plans, too many priorities' then when you complain you are seen as trouble.
Long standing staffing issues have been acknowledged and processes are now being put in place to address.

Lots of changes in the organisation over the last year or 2 have made the job more difficult and creates burn out.  Changes are based around meeting targets and are not in the best interest of the patients.  This is wrong and demoralising.
Loved the change from working at Alder Hey - fantastic organisation and [name removed] is just wonderful!

Main issue with my job is, we are always short staffed and expectations on DNs workload is increasing.  There is no maximum to the amount of patients referred to us so caseloads are getting bigger without extra staff to support.  Caseload in our area is getting bigger and more complex.  When there is a day were workload appears manageable staff are moved to help other teams therefore there is rarely an opportunity to catch up with non direct patient care.

Managers can often contradict each others narratives on practice and policy.  For example, KPI 's etc.  Difficulties within team environment and actions have not been followed up.  Professional capacity which results in no protected time for admin and poses risk for incidents.  Professionals in lower bands feel they are not acknowledged and decisions are always made by leadership.  Managers are not visible in the office and rarely engage with the team when passing etc.  Managers close office door and this often feels they are shut away from team.  Team canvas not been updated considering more than half of staff are new to service.
Many challenges with systems, duplication of reporting on multiple systems that don't communicate.  Challenges with core functions e.g. getting timely responses from HR.

MCFT is the best NHS trust I have worked for and it led by strong Exec Team who I have faith/trust has the best vision, plans, workstreams set for MCFT boroughs, families and services.  I am excited re: new much needed changes and developments, in particular MCFT Trusts commitment to supporting the most vulnerable complex families in our communities.
Mersey Care are the worst organisation I have worked for, they have staff working on unsafe staffing levels every day.  Staff are burnt out and ready to leave.  Mersey Care can't retain their staff.
Mersey care have been supportive to me throughout my employment here, I am enjoying working for Mersey care.

Mersey care is a trust that is only focused on meeting commissioned targets, the organisation does not value it's staff and there appears to be too many managers of B8 Who have no knowledge or skills of the staff they are managing and enforce working practices and SOPS that hamper the trained staff on completing there roles effectively.  Too much focus on supervision sessions and monitoring staff activity, rather then supporting staff to complete there roles or employing more staff to support services to work more effectively.  Mersey care does not care about staff, only about giving an outward appearance of being all singing and dancing without any merit.
Mersey Care is all about mental health all the other departments i.e. childrens.  Adults are not thought about or given as much consideration.

Mersey Care is not a very nice or good company to work in.  I am not even sure its genuinely NHS or a private company who has paid the NHS for a contract to run a service.  I've come to understand Its very good at paying lip service to looking after people and making out it cares but its actions are very different.  You would have to work in it to truly understand what I mean and go through all the subtle and not so subtle behaviours of higher management and other colleagues and how you get treated.  We've been moved around so much and how now we've been all lumped together different departments all squashed in a small space the noise can be unbearable and make it impossible to concentrate on what you are doing.  The things you hear, the complaints the stress the anxiety the axe to grind the all too personal conversations the tone and upset in peoples voices not having a moments peace without someone talking no matter where you go - the building we are in is not fit for purpose and is very unpleasant to work in.  There's no safe space and there's is nowhere to go for a private conversation without risk being overheard there aren't enough rooms for private meetings and booking one has become are real trial.  I would like to see the person who decided that this was a good idea come and work where we do and suffer it day in and day out.  We must have 50 - 100 people walk past our desks every day, its sounds as if they are walking on a wooden floor an irritating creaking noise all the time.  Just think about that for a moment, every single day the noise of people walking up and down past where you work, conversations going on all around you complaining, hearing the sound of their voices the tone the harshness in the voice and then having to do your job at the same time dealing with patients how they can be complaining, upset.  And I think that's it - decisions being made and it not thought out how it will effect people - stick them all together save money on rent of all other buildings let them suffer and then have to leave and we will just get someone else.  This is only going to lead to low morale stress people going off sick - leading to poor patient service and complaints, I was told that higher management gave the okay for another department to use another departments desks if they needed to without asking that department!  The first they new about it was when it started happening - that could of lead to a row the consequences could of been ever lasting and the person who said it was okay would never of suffered for it other people would of.
Mersey care need to concentrate on the services they currently provide and ensure they are working 100% before acquiring more companies as staff and patients are starting to suffer.

Mersey Care needs a better approach to work and home life balance not having fear at requesting flexible working hours with the threat you could be moved just because it fits the trusts' requirements of where another person is needed with in the trust, there should be more colleagues being able to do long days not just one person in a team that has the long days.  Waiting tools need to be looked at in regards to writing up/paper work needs to be added on to the waiting as writing up, care plans, schedules take time.

Mersey Care needs to concentrate on its existing portfolio rather than taking new services on - like care homes, which they cant staff and then disrupt established teams to poach staff to work in them - often out of their scope of practice.
Mersey Care NHS Foundation Trust makes me feel supported through various ways.

Merseycare are an uncaring organisation.  It has become more of a business rather than a patient focused NHS.  Merseycare want staff over worked and pushed beyond their boundaries.  Staff moral is very low, staff have become very demotivated and looking for alternative jobs.

Merseycare do not care about the staff which is evident with the sickness policy.  This policy is disgusting for staff who work tirelessly for the patients in all weathers and all environments (community staff).  Staff are penalised for being sick and have to adhere to strict stages and the fear of losing your job, who is never sick in 12 months?  This causes more stress and anxiety with staff.  Community staff are just expected to use their own cars and although we get mileage back its not the same amount, the miles are reduced to what we actually travel and they expect staff to have copious amounts of equipment in their cars.  They do not pay for wear and tear on the vehicles either.  Staff morale is at an all time low.  I believe Mersey don't care need to focus on the services/buildings/staff they have before buying anymore that cannot be run/staff.

Merseycare is a great organisation to work for.  It is very supportive of its staff and does its best to learn from any mistakes.  It does ask quite a bit at times and this can be quite stressful.  All in all a good employer.
Merseycare when it comes down to it is more interested in its own reputation and survival than it is in users.  I hope this will change with the new chief executive.
Micro managing can be counter productive as increases stress for staff and thus increases sickness.

Middle management will not consider flexible working as there are not enough staff to in teams to provide the service.  The budget is more important than patients.  Staff are punished and penalised if there is a waiting list yet they are working hard and their pleas for more staff are ignored because of the budget.  There are gaps in service provision where the Commissioners have not considered Physiotherapy for Learning Disability pts in South Sefton, Merseyside.
More recognition, more support, more staff, better wages.
More staff are needed to do our jobs effectively.  We are not robots and burnout is rife amongst the trust.  You can see this in the sickness rates and staff turnover.
More staff would be number one but most of the time we just have to get on with it.

MSC demand a high standard which if good.  However the expectations of how long task / plans etc take to implement is unrealistic.  This leaves staff running about in their own time regularly, feeling as it they are not effective enough Mandatory training rains in without warning because MSC wants something doing, having moved most to online putting the onus and all the pressure on staff for not completing, so often staff do in their family time.  While not considering the time these modules take to complete and the impact of poorly designed packages and failing IT server issues on clinical time / pressures.  Then MSC harp on about work life balance.  It bit more consideration and clear honest look at 1 element MT and how long this takes to complete may ensure senior managers plan more efficiently.

Mt team and manager are very supportive and fair and a pleasure to work with.  I had an incident with some offensive and sexually inappropriate behaviour from a patient and my manager supported me so well however I felt the way the issue was dealt with by Mersey Care was not sufficient or supportive.  I was unable to access health and wellbeing services in an appropriate way that would fit round my job role and have had no further input from them which was disappointing.
My answers are in relation to other partners such as LA, not just MCFT.

My current line manager and team manager at the time of this survey are supportive when I raise work pressures and time constraints.  This varies between different team managers and line managers.  There has been a huge increase in level of need with Speech and Language amongst children and this is very difficult to manage without more staff and in fact being understaffed.

My current team are amalgamating with other teams at present, it is a period of unsettled feelings and also whether funding will be approved for another year, I am concerned about placements I would be offered if funding was not approved.  Presently I still feel like we are working in as individual teams rather than a team as a whole.

My direct line manager is amazing and is one of the only reasons I remain in my current role.  They are beyond supportive and I feel they are an excellent manager.  I have previously been burnt out and had time off for this.  I did not feel ready to come back, but had no choice but to do so.  My manager has been as supportive as possible, but I feel I am already reaching burnout again.  We are understaffed, underpaid and underappreciated.  We are put in situations we shouldn't have to go into and we are expected to go into palliative dying patients and have no support following deaths, we are just expected to carry on and go to the next patient.  There is no facility to have an outlet for issues we are experiencing at work in terms of having monthly meetings with a registered mental health professional to debrief on the situations we deal with.  The counselling provided through occupational health is not beneficial and I found it did not help me at all.  In our team we have a multitude of issues with regards to parking, with staff regularly having to pay out to park to see patients and/or getting fines.  This increases overall negativity and stress.  I feel stressed at work, but we don't receive enough pay for what we do and more and more expectations are put on us.  I have a lease vehicle and feel it is actually an extortionate amount for a vehicle.  I feel we are not considered as community staff who have to travel for work so our mileage has to be higher, in comparison to colleagues who just commute to and from work.  I like my team, but I live 15 miles away from base and the amount spent on petrol is just increasing.  The morale overall is shocking, we cannot retain staff and the boundaries different teams cover need to be established as it is stressful trying to handover out of area patients, with other teams refusing even though they have patients on their caseloads in that area.

My flexible working application was rejected on the base that I don't have kids below 6 years old when was accepted for others who does have kids older then 6 years old - discrimination.  Rotation is not implemented, some people can work from home and some not - issue raised multiple times but not actioned.  Lack of appropriate desks and chairs - have to sit b broken table and armchair - MSK issues - raised with management multiple times - no action.  Management is dismissal and doesn't listen concerns and doesn't consult decisions with team members.

My HR27 is frequently not adhered to, I am told if I have annual leave I forfeit my working from home day (although this day is due to my disability). My work pace does not make reasonable adjustments re my disability, I am advised if I have dizziness episodes then I have to go off sick, instead of being able to work from home, as it is part of my disease.  I do not feel valued by my management team, despite the fact I can be left alone every week currently due to short staffing, on the day they suggested I work from home.  I waited months for a written HR27 to be put in place.  Our management are obstructive and unapproachable.  They denied us the right to go to panel to put our case for a band 3, so a grievance had to go in, which we won, but now they are dragging their heels about us going to panel.  This has been going on for years.

My immediate manager is fantastic [name removed] takes initiative to ensure the team they are working with are well both physically and mentally, using there time effectively to ensure we are all able to speak to them when we need, ensuring we are all okay and feel valued within the team.  [name removed] is the manager we all go to and the reason many SPOA staff are still working with the team.

My immediate team is great and very supportive, however senior management and the trust are less supportive and we feel like just a number to cover shifts rather than actual people.  We feel listened to but rarely do we feel we have had our concerns acted upon and we're just listened to to placate us.  I would recommend to work with my team and for family to come to where I work however I would not recommend the organisation as an employer.  I have had external training opportunities declined numerous times and feel like there isn't the support for growth in my job in terms of learning and training to enable me to be confident in my knowledge to stay up to date on developments and guidelines for my role.

My job has improved over the past 6 months, I feel this is due to new manager who is very supportive.  The survey does not capture change in management that can either have a negative or effect on our job roles.  If new manager was not in post my answers would have been very different and less positive.  I would suggest questions regarding new management to capture this information.

My line manager is wonderful but their hands are tied by management with any issues we have.  Long term absences are not covered by Bank or Agency staff, even during annual leave!  My job has been made even harder during and after COVID with staff working from home, it added extra work because they were not in the office to deal with it themselves.  It is harder to contact staff via phone or email.  It can take several calls to different member's of staff in one organisation to make contact, if at all.  And it is the same with emails.  There was a proposal recently for staff to return to the office, but it is at managers discretion, so not much has changed.

My manager who sits under the community Service in South Sefton does not know how to support  staff that has neuro-diverse brain; they have received some work place training and whilst initially, it was being met with understanding, it became apparent that management were not willing to meet accommodations in practice - it was all just written down in a piece of paper but never really supported.  I have a fantastic colleagues and our Trust is great but we have an unreliable line manager, where they change their statements, they don't do what they say they do, and they see feedback as a threat and take things personally.  In turn, the team morale is low in general and those who has neuro diverse brain were covertly discriminated.

My only worry at the moment is there is a chance our service might be coming to an end in [date removed], this is a big worry for me and adds stress to my life as I worry what the future may hold.  I also am a carer so work some days from home, I worry if I need to find a new job going forward I might not have the option to work from home and I don't know how I would be able to manage this.

My organisation is strongly focused on reporting which takes a great deal of time away from direct patient care on a daily basis.  The organisation is introducing a vast amount of changes which is hard to keep up with.  Several Pilot schemes have been introduced within my team without any prior information being disseminated or a discussion from/with the senior managers.

My primary concern is that there doesn't seem to be any kind of forward planning to address issues that are or are likely to occur.  There is a tendency to let situations develop and deal with the fallout rather than having potential solutions in place.  Recruitment seems to take an interminable amount of time to resolve.  We have a post that was vacated 17 months ago and still has not been filled with a permanent appointment.
My team and manager are fantastic- if it wasn't from outside pressures of other teams/levels of safeguarding I would not consider moving job roles.

My team has recently joined with other services so will have a new management structure going forward.  The information provided is generally about my pervious small team.  Unsure yet it things will be as good in the future.  Hopefully they will.

My team is classed as being fully staffed but the team is running at full capacity day in and day out.  The majority of staff are still on EMIS catching up on documentation in the evenings as even the band [number removed] take a list of visits on a high weighting.  It feels unsafe and it feels as though patients are not getting thorough assessments.  Incidents happen and quite often the reason behind them is lack of staff but nothing seems to change.  The quota for staff seems dated but the complexity of patients and the diversity of the area that we cover is very demanding.  I feel that if staff and patients were valued then staffing levels would be increased to ensure that patient centred care could be carried out.
My team is extremely micromanaged which often makes the job challenging at times.
My team is very supportive and welcoming.
Negative comments that have been made in this are in relation to my previous job at another NHS Trust.

Nepotism is a culture here.  As is poor behaviour - bullying / micromanagement and obsession with assurance to tick meaningless boxes of nonsense - real issues are not dealt with.  We state we support flexible working - yet have very senior managers openly stating 'it is frowned upon here' and life is made as hard as possible for anyone who has a flexible arrangement.  I rarely get any supervision and have not had a PACE review for over 4 years.  My manager is too bust trying to please his own manager - and the limited time we every gt is wasted by him looking at his watch and phone continually.  This is not helpful or supportive.

NHS management needs to stop continuously attempting to increase the staff work load.  If demands on services are increasing then this should be managed by increasing staffing.  We are continuously being informed that managers are scrutinising your diaries and that we are expected to see a minimum number of patients every day regardless of patient complexity.  Without completing admin outside of work hours it is impossible to maintain a case load.  Support services are disappearing resulting in increasing numbers of complex patients with social problems or safeguarding issues being routinely referred to the service.  Management focus on waiting lists can never be resolved by increasing pressure on existing staff or without investing in new staff and retaining existing staff.  I have worked in the NHS for over [number removed] years and have never witnessed such a hollowed out and demoralised NHS as exists now.
NHS should focus on giving opportunity to individuals that are good, want to develop, and will make NHS and the patient happy.  Not focusing solely on the DEI project.
No communication, emails and queries unanswered.  Lack of support.  Lots of negativity.

No continuity with leadership teams, too many management changes, too frequently which impacts on creating a cohesive teams.  Listening to other Mersey care colleagues who verbalise lack of support, increased work loads and not listened to.
No further comments thanks.
No I am happy with working condition at the moment.
None at present, We have a new amazing Team leader at present.

Nursing has changed so much over the years- I have been a nurse for many years and used to love my job.  Policies have taken over nurses using their own clinical judgement and I feel newly qualified staff have a fear of everything and feel they have to do absolutely everything in one consultation with their patients for fear of missing something and being penalised- I feel this will lead to burn out at a much earlier age as they all appear so stressed.

Ongoing issues with staffing which means extra work for us on top of our caseload.  Very frequent staff sickness which I feel sometimes some colleagues can take very last minute sickness and possibly abuse the sickness system- taking sickness when not necessary suitable to e.g. childcare issues whilst other members of staff go above and beyond to ensure this is not an issue and are more committed to their role.
Opportunity for home working would greatly improve my work-life balance.
Our pay is crap.
Our team would be better able to support our patients if we had more permanent staff and did not have to rely on bank staff.

Over the last year the service has changed and working hours, this had a massive impact on myself due to childcare responsibility's.  I personally felt that although the trust said you don't have to change your hours, that was not genuine.  We were put through multiple meetings with senior managers and told we had to be flexible and questioned on family dynamics and why we couldn't comply with change of working hours.  Given dates from senior mangers when I would have an answer to flexible working request for them not to reply by this deadline and me have to chase them for a response.  I spent months thinking I would have to leave a job that I love as was unable to comply with change of hours as no childcare.  I feel as a mental health trust the communication has been terrible and not caring about there staff one bit.

Over the years the bar has been gradually lowered and the standard required at each band has drastically lowered resulting in large discrepancies in nursing care.  More priority is put on certificates gained rather than relevant and appropriate experience for the role.  This is evident at every level within the service.

Over worked short staffed under valued and community district nursing needs investment and a revamp for it to carry on being fit for purpose in the future to carry on looking after a more complex ageing community, and staff working with out getting dinner breaks and proper rest periods.

Overall a good place to work, only negatives is financial.  The clinics have poor equipment and wage is poor.  Supportive colleagues and management.  Flexible working is a bonus.  Workload seems to be increasing, documentation wise, put more a strain on the job it's self.
Overall.  Really like working for my team and the wider Mersey Care.  I have liked working in my team from day 1, which was 7 years ago.
People with disabilities are not treated reasonably or equitably and the Trust is enrolled as a Disability Friendly Trust.
Phlebotomy seem to be forgotten about as not even listed on the job roles and often feel ignored and undervalued.
Please allow flexible working e.g. work from home.

Please can the sickness policy be reviewed, it does not promote being off ill when needed.  When on a stage, you feel forced to come into work if you are unwell.  Could the days of sickness not be calculated over your entire employed time with the Trust, not over the last year?  It can be very stressful knowing if your unwell you will be escalated to the next stage, when you are genuinely unwell.
Please implement e health roster so we can see our shifts.  Please make longer timeslots for patients so we don't rush, ESP for new patients onboarding.

Podiatry are sometimes not considered as a high profile department.  I think this is very sad.  The team work together and support each other.  The culture of care towards each other and our patients is very evident.  I [details removed] soon and will miss everyone.
Poor leadership.  Not able to speak up.  Challenging.  Not listened to.  Very defensive.

Poor staffing in other areas impacts upon areas where staffing is good which in turn causes an impact to the services that have the appropriate staff to do their work.  The constant request for staff from our service affects the staffing in our team which increases stress, impacts our KPI, effects the service service provision and prevents us from achieving set targets.
Pressure of workload, demands for managerial, clinical and professional duties can be difficult to manage some days.

PSIRF and all the associated paperwork is clogging up valuable clinical time which would be better served with patients.  Experienced clinicians are processing RADARs, undertaking reviews then reviewing reviews.  It is a cumbersome process involving a multitude of personnel who would be better served treating patients and being available in their departments.  Nobody is questioning the value of patient safety but recently it has become unmanageable and is having a detrimental effect on staff morale.
Pt lists can be very busy were we do not have time for dinner very stressful.
Questions about managers have been answered in relation to my direct manager/line manager not senior management i.e. band 8 manager as most of my negative comments are in relation to these managers.

Same comments every year.  Nothing changes.  No progression unless friends with senior managers.  Interview's often unfair in that they already know who is getting the job.  Or, in my case; didn't get the job because a newly qualified middle aged person could remember policies off the top of their head, whereas, I and menopausal and struggled with that.  Never mind the fact that quoting policies is rote learning, rather than genuine understanding of policies and how to use them.  10 years of doing my job well has led to nothing but disappointment and now genuine apathy.

Same issues never resolved within the team.  Under staffed constantly, relying on bank and historically agency to stay afloat.  Very complex patients.  Escalating for more than 3 years the same concerns to operational managers yet we are still struggling with no sign of improvement.  Staff retention is poor due to working conditions in this trust within District Nursing.  Operational managers with no District Nursing background overseeing the service when they don't understand the sheer amount of extra work we put in.
School health is never listed as a choice in registered nurses.

Senior management make decisions about organisational changes which directly affect my role.  They will often invite us to meetings but job pressures occasionally prevent attendance.  Decisions are often made despite our objections.  Senior management continue to expand the organisation by taking on roles from other organisations, without adequately assessing the impact this will have on the staff whom are expected to undertake those roles.

Senior management within the NHS Talking therapies team leadership approach has been extremely poor with regards to their communication towards the team during several meetings this year.  There is a top down do as I say approach which is damaging morale and leading to widespread distrust.  This type of leadership style will result in staff leaving and it is no surprise that there are significant staff vacancies.  I do not believe that their communication aligns with the trust values in the few times they have been present in staff meetings.  There is a tunnel vision approach to achieving 50% recovery.  I would remind leadership that reliable improvement scores make a substantial difference to the day to day lives of many of our clients.
Senior managers are out of touch about the needs of the patients - too focused on numbers.

Sickness rules are not the same for everyone, lateness is not treated the same for everyone, some people make time up some people come and go as they please.  Holiday entitlement is not treated fairly, some staff work from home in emergency and some staff won't as they refuse to even though they have equipment at home.

Since we have had a new management structure, we now have a Operational Service Manager and Professional Lead whereas before we had a Clinical Lead who was my line manager and provided me with regular clinical supervision.  This means I do not have regular clinical supervision and my line manager is not from my own profession.  The suggested resolution to this is to do peer supervision which is not always adequate or appropriate.
So many more expectations / training to complete e.g. higher risk management expectations, rather than refer to secondary for their input.

Social Work is undervalued within this Organisation and we are a vital cog in the discharge wheel.  We lead and guide our health colleagues and the team do often feel their work is unrecognised.  There is no ability for team leaders to progress to Band 8A level other than moving to a different area of work outside social work, this has been sanctioned by senior managers.  It is often sole destroying to witness the increase in operational management when SW are left behind regardless of the heavy workload which is wrapped around with Legislation and law.
Some of the questions are weird.
Some times the organisation care about ticking boxes rather than their patients and staff.
Something to note on the qualifications I have dual qualifications, nursing and social worker.  Please add that on the survey drop box.  Thank you.

Staff morale within the service has deteriorated since the trust was taken over by a much larger trust.  Management no longer interacts in a positive way and does not involve staff in decision making about the service.
Staff services are poor in this trust - recruitment, payroll, HR.

Staffing levels are the biggest problem we face.  Recruitment and retention of staff is extremely difficult / poor thus putting more pressure on existing staff.  The real concerns of staff are not being listened to, in Podiatry the eligibility criteria is at least 10 years out of date.  We should be operating a High Risk service only with the levels of staff we have available, yet low risk patients have easy access to NHS assessment / treatment when they should be directed to private practitioners.  Meaningful change is being delayed by transformation and IT systems remain unreliable and time-consuming.
Stating my age / gender / job title does not make this survey anonymous.
Stressed workforce, no staff, staff off sick.  Limited communication from senior managers, they change all the time and we don't know who they are especially from band 8 level upwards.

Talking Therapies really needs to hire more therapists as the waiting lists are far too long and staff are always leaving which puts more pressure on existing staff.  I think the only way to do this would be to look at increasing the pay banding particularly for psychological wellbeing practitioners as we are very underpaid therefore not many people stay in the job role for long.
Targets appear to have become a priority over everything else which has a negative impact on staff and patient's.

Teams within the locality need to provide more support to the district nursing service.  The demand on district nurses is like never before.  It is driving staff away and when raised to higher management no feedback is received.

Thank you for allowing a recent away day with our team.  We had a really good day and it helped me appreciate my colleagues and line managers efforts in providing a thoughtful, low cost activity that we could all take part in and enjoy time together away from the work environment.
Thank you NHS.

The amount of defensive practice happening now due to fear in clinicians is truly heart-breaking.  I have never in [timeframe removed] seen such anxiety amongst a staff group.  Too many box ticking exercises being placed onto Mersey care staff and not enough recognition for the documentation that covers all these box ticking areas/exercises.  This is taking up far too much clinical time.  There is too much recruitment to managerial positions and too many of these jobs are with people who have no or minimal clinical experience working in the NHS today, and it shows.  Whilst the main areas are ignored the real problems will sadly not be solved.  I will always remain proud to work in the NHS but feel sad that decision making processes that pave the way for the future can present as ill informed at times.
The amount of staff being on long term sick, particularly managers, is worrying.  Far too many people use sickness as an excuse to not do their jobs whilst others have to pick up the slack.

The current incident reporting system Radar in my opinion is not fit for purpose for community services and is related to impatient care so we are faced with a lot of irrelevant questions and is confusing for community staff to complete it they actually manage to complete form!  This issue has been raised with our managers.  We have recently been asked to complete a work plan which is basically time in motion and we were shown previous results led by therapies in a hospital setting that cannot relate to community services.  I personally felt this only added to stress levels in myself in that I was under performing.  Clearly you would be able to spend more face to face time with patients in a ward setting than in the community.  We were also given the breakdown for banding i.e. band 4 and 5 in a hospital setting would have 95% face to face time as they have no other clinical responsibilities i.e. supervision of other staff.  In reality a band 4 and 5 in the community in our team is not allocated only north, south or central localities but visits all, so this would incur increased travel time.  I felt, and aware other team members felt same, doing a presentation that was completed in a hospital setting was not a true representation for community services and I felt under unfair pressure due to this.  Our work in community is very different, we are not supplying equipment urgent for discharge but working in the home setting which is very different in respect to increased time for the patients we see, waiting for adaptations from housing associations in some instances.  Can take as long as 6 months and as a result these patients remain on our caseloads.

The demands from the Trust for ever increasing documentation/templates to be completed weighs heavily on the staff, increasing time pressures with patient appointment slot's - as we are also finding patients are presenting with more complex needs and problems.  This needs addressing as it is the same issues that would make staff want to leave their job.  Practice Nurses are very task orientated and do not delve or deal with patient's additional problems during their consultations?

The department does not have enough staff for the demands of the service.  External training courses (once per year) have been cancelled due to low staffing levels/ service demands - Lost CPD.  Service not very flexible on leave for last minute school meetings.  Occupational health times very long but were still expected to work when we have our own health issues.  Too many people on home visits, staff are going to have long term health problems.  A lot would be able to get clinic.

The governance aspect of the job is stifling service development.  I recognise its importance, but so much of my time at work feels like it is taken up 'gathering evidence' - for QRV, CQC monitoring, PSIRF, surveys etc.  Constantly having to evidence that we are performing well ironically feels like it has a negative impact on team performance.  It so often takes me away from looking after patients /direct care and training for my team.  I wish there was a more suitable way to provide quality assurance without the masses of documentation.  I often have to spend time at home completing g this type of documentation.

The HR department in our organization is not doing enough to address serious concerns regarding staff members who continually put patient care at risk.  While we are often reminded to follow existing policies, this approach fails to tackle the root issues at hand.  The HR team's excessive risk aversion limits their ability to effectively manage these critical situations, leaving employees frustrated and patients vulnerable.  It's essential that HR takes a more proactive stance to ensure a safe and supportive environment for both staff and patients.

The internationally recruited nurses in the community care are rarely given opportunity to progress.  When applying for jobs you get feedbacks that you don't have experience but you have answered all questions well.  Some places even failed to send you feedback on an interview you did.
The job I do is non patient facing.  The actual work is fine, however I do really feel staff are not listened to.  There is little opportunity for career development.

The lack of support, the lack of insight into/understanding of team needs and the very poor communication from senior management team (8a/8b/8c and higher) is just appalling, disheartening and demoralising.  The core values of Mersey Care have been severely lacking in the past year by our senior management team.

The leadership team is in disarray, therefore the team comes across as dysfunctional.  No one knows who does what role and people are reluctant do undertake tasks or roles that were previously the responsibility of staff in 'higher' bands..  The team is also incredibly hierarchal..  Some experienced staff do not like being questioned and very defensive about being asked questions, rather than understanding that questions are not criticism, questions are a quest for understanding and learning.

The main cause for concern is the work load and unrealistic expectations in district nursing. I spend many hours completing my paperwork from home instead of spending time doing homework or spending time with my child.  The work load has caused many arguments in my personal.  Life with my partner as he feels I'm always working and never present.
The main issues at present is low pay for the responsibility held, skill level used, and qualifications obtained.
The morale of the team is so low because of band 8a.  They disregard people's needs and holds grudges.

The NHS as a whole is in crisis, as we are all very much aware.  After 30 plus years as a registered nurse I have seen my role and responsibilities increase dramatically from that for which I was trained, and without the motivating financial reward.  Commissioning means trusts are "chasing pots of gold" and spreading staff across services resulting in increased workloads and reductions in ability to provide outstanding care.  If a service costs for example £50, it can not be implemented for £20, and this is happening everywhere.  If it cant be done properly then we need to be allowed to do what we can do, to a high standard.  Sounds simple, because it kind of is?  .  Stop saying yes and learn to say No?

The only concern I have is that the trust has a uniform policy that is outdated.  We work with children who are in care and live with foster carers, family members or residential settings.  A lot of the children struggle with being in care and having nurses visiting them in uniform immediately draws attention to them that they don't want.  Some children specifically ask for us not to wear a uniform so we don't stand out when we are visiting the home but the trust is not listening to these children and ordering us to wear a uniform.  I bring this up every year and despite the voice of the child and you said we did campaigns this is not being listened to, we are happy to wear the uniform when required but we want to build meaningful relationships with the children and young people but this is difficult when we are seen to be not listening to their wishes.

The only time I feel drained in my job is when I work with another team 3 months a year.  I love the job but travel to work 9 hours driving in 3 days.  But I understand this is part of my job and it is only 3 months out of the year.

The organisation appears to be pre-occupied with administration and governance as opposed to direct patient care.  Despite highlighting unsafe practices on a number of occasions, including via the Radar system, the organisation and in particular the department persists in continuing with initiatives and projects that are detrimental to patients and staff.  Staff are not supported clinically in an effective way to deliver these initiatives and this puts patients in direct danger.

The organisation focuses too much on 'business' rather than patient care.  There are inequalities in the organisation between divisions and overtime/additional staffing approval and this is not consistent across services.  I feel the size of the organisation has an impact on staff experience and staff feeling valued.  The trust continue to take on additional responsibilities when we are unable to safely staff the existing services.

The organisation has expanded greatly and the changes come in on a frequent basis making it hard to keep up with all the changes.  Service specs change and the information we get about them is limited until it can be seen that we are not meeting KPI's.  There are an increasing number of managers and line management changes regularly which does not give any continuity.  In this rapidly changing occupational climate it's not the nicest place to work.
The organisation has removed admin and not replaced it appropriately.  Trained nurses are expected to under take admin duties which is not a good resource of their time.

The organisation is now too big, corporate functions including HR are not resourced to support clinical services fully and corporate functions lie with clinical managers who don't always have that skill set amongst everything they need to do.  Plus some corporate functions are still unknown to clinical operational services plus there is at times a cost attached to clinical services for these functions when teams are not always fully established or demand outweighs capacity in a lot of clinical services.

The organisation makes too many changes such as formularies, policies, procedures etc.  It is hard to learn what you need to do and then learn again just as you have got used to something.  Some changes do not make sense or work, for example taking off very effective dressings away from the formulary and replacing them with another dressing which is very cheap and does not work.  Training opportunities are lacking, not enough on or get cancelled.  Also some are too far and staff unable to get there due to short staffing.  5-8 working new to district nursing is not working as it should.  In the teams there are very few people that can cover this and it is putting strain on the ones that are doing these shift patterns.  Many of us are working from home after shifts or on days off as the workload is too much.  We are told that we should be able to complete this in the shift but there is no way due to capacity.  Job roles are not desirable such as band 6 district nursing role, too much pressure, lack of support etc.  Staffing levels are poor, certain teams are struggling more than others and the other teams are pulled to support, ongoing issue which never seems to be getting better.  Skill mixes within teams also poor, again putting pressure on people.  Some staff have been treated poorly by the organisation such as not keeping their promise regarding permanent jobs following secondment.  Being told to move to other teams as job not available in their own team.  Some staff not having support from the organisation following incidents such as bullying allegations etc.  Some staff have received more support than other people due to lack of competency/skills and they continue to work in the trust and still not up to scratch many months/years later.  The trust is not doing enough to appropriately deal with situations like this.

The organisation needs to identify and offer in-house training and a clear career progression for it's admin staff.  The general feeling amongst my colleagues is that although individual clinical staff do value our role and the support we provide to enable THEM to be more effective, the organisation needs to focus more on the development of quality admin staff to attract more young people into the role.  The cohort is currently split between older staff who are very experienced but close to retirement age and young staff who have fallen into the role without quality foundational training and procedural rigour prior to recruitment.  A "grow your own" approach with admin staff would greatly benefit the organisation and the wider NHS.

The organisation seems to have a priority of acquiring new services and expanding its foothold on services without due care in how to improve the services they currently have or the effect it has on the staff.  Staff seem bottom of the list on priorities for a number of years with executives unaware of how their decisions affect frontline staff.  Mersey doesn't care is a byword for how staff have to referred to the organisation for many years and known by staff from other trusts.  I have worked for the trust before foundation trust status and left due to the poor communication from managers and executives who also did not take staff concerns seriously, I returned due to limited opportunities in my chosen field without moving out of area.  Whilst I have seen improvements in staff opportunities to advance their careers with apprenticeships, poor senior leadership, poor recruitment, staff shortages and unrealistic expectations are putting staff and service users at risk.

The progression opportunities within the trust Psychology professions without core professions are very limited as is the access to external training.  It would be great if the trust implemented more apprenticeship opportunities to widen access to the psychology professions which are so sought after within teams and there appears to be a consistent shortage.

The restructuring of the SLT band 8a and above has had a detrimental effect on the workings of the team.  We have less band 8a time and the duties they performed have filtered down to the band 7s.  There is no appreciation of this from SLT and there is an underlying atmosphere that the Band 7s are not working hard enough; we are told repeatedly that our diaries are being scrutinised and monitored.  I took an instance of incivility from a member of the team to both the band 8a and 8b and no action has been taken, resulting in me feeling unsafe in work.  It was suggested to me, by my band 8a, to request carers leave to take my child for surgery.  After the request was made, I was told I would be able to take 4 hours and have to work the other 4 hours back as TOIL - something that I struggle to do.  If I knew this was the case I would've asked to change my NWD.  This does not make me feel valued by the organisation.

The senior leadership teams need to better role model flexible working as it still seems to be frowned upon.  Senior leaders need to reduce the amount of time spent at V7 and get out into services on a weekly basis.

The service is poorly run and staff concerns are not addressed effectively.  Burn out is normalised and staff expected to just put up with it as they are told that that is how the role is.  Senior management do not care about staff wellbeing and repeatedly make decisions about service changes that put further strain on staff wellbeing without even consulting them.  Changes to the service are being made frequently which confuses staff and mean that there is never any consistency as to what we are meant to do.  Staff are expected to work beyond their remit and their training and beyond the limits of their accreditation.  Staff are told that they should work outside of their remit and essentially not record it on the system so that the service can still maintain accreditation.  Staff morale is very low and burn out is very high and the service is doing nothing about it because they care more about the targets than the staff who are burning themselves out trying to reach them.

The service no longer is no longer a joy to work in.  It's no longer about people, it's about numbers and statistics.  Too much is expected of us in terms of information gathering and responsibility, this takes over doing the actual job.  I feel like a case manager and not a therapist.  We do make a difference to people's lives but unless their outcome measures show certain numbers then it doesn't count!  I came into the profession to help people, now I consistently feeling that I am just managing risk and seeing people that are our service is not suitable for.  Yet we are still expected to get results!

The sickness/absence policy is too lenient.  We have staff who repeatedly are on long term sick leave and remain in work.  When these staff are in leadership posts and several are off at a time it is difficult to function effectively.

The support and encouragement from my manager is second to none, not only to myself, but my work colleagues also.  Unfortunately, I feel that the same cannot be said about the trust.  In my opinion, they are so focussed on the business and financial side of things, that the staff are often left misguided, misinformed (or without information) and appear to be wanting to take on the world.

The team I work for are lovely and support each other well.  However with recent changes, requested administrative tasks make you repeat your work in several different formats, leading to unnecessary and excessive work loads and it is ineffective use of practitioners time.  Our team managers are also lovely and very supportive.  Whilst the trust advocate being able to report issues upwards, often it feels very much like a top-down process, in which changes occur in our service decided by management who aren't part of our team and with no discussion on the matter first.  When we report concerns around any of these changes, our service managers take this seriously but we get the sense that not much changes when raised higher.

The team I work is excellent - full of dedicated professionals and extremely supportive of one another.  The Trust, however, less so, with the impression that we need to 'tick boxes' over and above providing patient care.  The Trust aim of "perfect care" sets us up to fail - we are expected to deliver perfection, despite imperfect circumstances and situations.  The question regarding accessibility to healthy, nutritious food appears to be essentially asking us if we can prepare a healthy, nutritious packed lunch.  At our current work site we DO NOT have any access to healthy, nutritious food on site, unless we prepare it ourselves and bring it with us.
The trust just doesn't seem to care about employees.
The Trust needs to improve its culture and improve speaking up regarding current and previous complaints.

The uncertainty of not knowing if the service will be recommissioned for 2025 is very frustrating and damaging to staff wellbeing and morale.  We provide a high class service to our patients and are passionate about the service and intervention we provide and this should be recognised with basic communication from commissioners regarding the service moving forwards.  Not only staff but patients are very anxious regarding this.

The wider trust seems to have lots of process issues.  For example being told a department cannot do something despite there being instructions on how to do it on the department's own website.  There does not seem to be considerations for parents of young children, or parents whose children have additional complex needs, in regards to carers leave or ad hoc flexible working.  There seems to be an increase in duplications of recording, and unnecessary processes, in comparison to a few years ago.
There are a lot of things within the Trust that need addressing.

There are to many process and people involved in simple decision making in order to develop practice; this can be frustrating having to have multiple presentations/conversations outside of the agreed governance process to satisfy a senior colleagues need to be involved.  A governance process is clear and explained, and then there are 2 to 3 more that are introduced along the process with an insinuation a 'process' hasn't been followed.  This is demoralising for teams.  Alongside this Roles and responsibilities are not clear, as above you think you have went to the correct channels and people for approval/consultation and then a new role is introduced and/or the person in a role feels like they should have been involved and the process starts again.

There are too many layers of management.  There is an encouraged culture of bullying from managers.  I have had my hours cut by the management.  I feel so poor working for Mersey Care.  I use all my annual leave for medical appointments and sometimes my manager will not let me attend my medical appointments due to "service needs".  I have to request that my manager will let me go to the toilet, which is humiliating.  I had severe [condition removed] and requested to go home, I was told "get your [obscenity's removed] back to work".  The toxic environment between staff and management is insufferable.  Management just constantly boast about exotic holidays, what fancy car they are getting, while the staff at the bottom are lucky to have food and heating.  Mersey Care policies are only there to protect the Managers and make life awful for other staff.  Working for Mersey Care has had a negative impact on my physical and mental health.
There does not appear to be any opportunities for progression or flexible working when you are in a small team.  It is a constant top down approach with little or no consultation.

There have been many occasions where we have been extremely short staffed and under pressure due to staff sickness/leave, I often feel like we are not supported by the organisation at all during such occasions and staff have been left feeling stressed out and exhausted.

There is a different between my experience of my team and service that I work in compared to the trust as an organisation.
