I have found the trust to be largely unhelpful, defensive in practice, far removed from patient care, wellbeing and also staff wellbeing, poor approach to toil (encouraging staff to work unpaid hours) poor system development, poor management systems, poor escalation process, processes across services.  My sense is that the trust cannot cope with the size of itself and has become process for the sake of process at times, without clear rationale or consultation.  I have never heard that we can't do things or have to do things because that's just the way trust does it so much in another trust I've worked in.  I've worked in other trusts in my career and this is by far the worst.

There is a discrepancy between the approach taken between physical and mental health in culture.  There appears to be persistent behaviours from Liverpool Community Health that are embedded and affecting community health.  As such, the lauded Restorative and Just Learning Culture is not embedded if you don't fit the dominant culture.

There is a feeling from staff that senior leadership team do not fully respect AHP roles and the differences in ways of working from nursing teams.  There is a lack of staff engagement with changes driven top down without getting staff on board or listening to staff suggestions.  At times it feels like a blame culture - over scrutiny of caseloads and numbers rather than caring about quality of care or what staff are doing on visits/the complexity - numbers over quality.  No regard for complexity or lack of pathways and processes between teams to be more efficient in working.  Little feedback or appreciation shown towards therapy staff from the SLT within urgent care.  Immediate team and managers very supportive and care about quality of care and patient outcomes.

There is a serious issue with retention within the trust, especially of senior and experienced nurses'.  We are employing more junior staff without the necessary qualifications and support.  Management is very 'reactive'.  There is covert bullying - lots of micro aggressions.  Some HR employment policies are concerning - verbal feedback after interviews suggesting 'your sickness is awful'.  Suggestion that staff will be demoted if failing courses.  The job itself is lovely and the patient contact is great.  The managers and the trust are not.

There is currently lots of changes happening in the service I work.  This is exciting and should be a positive experience however the way in which these changes are managed and implemented leave staff feeling vulnerable and unsafe in practice.  The changes are often presented as they happen as in the day of or days before the change with minimal or vague information provided.  Change is often delivered via email and with a flow chart presented to follow.  Often no support or additional training is provided or access to relevant systems to enable to complete the new tasks expected.  The changes that are currently happening involve staff linking in with other services or working in new services with differing systems.  This has left staff feeling frustrate and inadequate.

There is currently no way for me to progress through the NHS in this role (Current band [number and Job title removed]).  Opportunities for career progression would be great as would enable me to stay within my service or the NHS and also provide the opportunity to enhance the service I currently provide to patients.

There is no follow through - staff members on stage 3 - no resolution and the staff remain causing undue pressure on teams.  Whilst we all want to be supported and support staff, the fact that HR policies aren't followed through to the end, leaves managers frustrated, with high levels of long term and short term sickness, that places a significant strain on teams/services.  HR should follow their own policies and listen to the managers of that service.

There is too much bureaucracy in the organisation that stifles innovation and meaningful change.  And not nearly enough empowered decision-making closer to clinical services.  As a senior clinical leader I really value my working relationships with operational colleagues but there really are too many layers of operational management.  Clinicians are tightly regulated however we have great disparity in how clinical staff and operational/strategic staff are held accountable.  It's hard to avoid poorly accountable ops colleagues who often don't feel empowered or are ill-equipped to make decisions.  There seems much more tendency towards a Command and Control as opposed to empowering devolved, accountable, decision making to colleagues where the information is at ground level.  Part of my own role is to ensure clinical governance is appropriate in the services I lead.  As an organisation I think we struggle to embed lessons learned from COVID where we were far more pragmatic.  We seem now to have extended past the point of optimal governance, which now hinders processes and makes change too arduous and complex to be useful.  We are constantly fed 'near miss' examples to make us vigilant but the same context is not applied on the flip side to great practice which seems to be celebrated, but not applied and embedded.  This makes us risk averse but indirectly 'quality averse' too.  Motivated and innovative staff in clinics that I lead are now preferring to 'simply turn up to work' as it's just too hard to make any meaningful change.  I should qualify this by saying these are not staff simply ground down by workload; work is tough, but it is the often 'off-target' and poorly supportive infrastructure around that grinds them down.

There is unrealistic expectations for the number of staff in comparison to the volume of work.  Within the organisation there is not a fair number of staff from one region to another when number of referrals to number of staff is compared.  This has been raised and nothing has changed.

There needs to be parity in development and career progression opportunities for AHPs, it does not exist at this time.  We are not a staff led organisation but a nurse led organisation and this is not in any way derogatory towards nurses, they are the backbone of the organisation, but AHPs deserve an equal opportunity at being the frontrunners and work shoulder to shoulder with nurses.
There should be a box were you can explain the reason you have given a certain answer.
Things seem to be improving.  Better managers.  Better culture.

Think we should be given the choice of working from home.  Pro are get more work done at home and less time travelling to office.  Sickness levels are down as you can just work from home if you have an off day when you just don't feel well.

This is a mental health trust that has acquired a large number of physical health services.  I feel the organisation focuses more on the mental health side.  They have built several new mental health buildings whilst they shunt the health staff from one worn out building to another.  We have been allocated smaller working spaces each and every time we have been moved.  My team in particular has been moved [number removed] times in the last [timeframe removed].  We used to be one large cohesive team but now we are all either working from home or locations that mean we cannot be together.  I am extremely unhappy.  The trust is scrutinising us on KPIs and the band 8s and above have no idea how complex our work is with extremely complex and challenging patients.  I am not really a [job title removed] anymore, I am a case manager and this is not what I joined the NHS for in the first place.  Our immediate band 8s are under such pressure.  One of them left their post after only [timeframe removed] stating that the role was impossible to do for one person.  They were replaced by another, who is saying the exact same thing.  How is that effective for the rest of the team?  .  Merseycare is too big of an organisation and before long something serious will happen with the physical health side of it because, largely due to the trust focusing on their mental health side.  And now job planning - basically a way of managers to tell us that we have to see x amount of patient in a day and x amount of new patients in a week.  This is not a caring organisation, this is a dictatorship!

This is the worst trust I have ever worked for in my [timeframe removed] of service within the NHS.  They have no regard for staff health/well being, they have continually lied to us face to face.  As soon as I can find another job I will leave.

This organisation has poor management and recruitment process;.  This organisation most of the time listen but has no actions or ignore suggestions from team leads regarding staffing and pressure on the workload.  Poor communication from Band 8's and above with clinical staff.  All services are understaffed.  Staff are leaving and no staff given to replace missing space.

This survey is more like a snap shot rather than a reflection of overall working life.  Currently we are extremely short staffed so feeing lots of extra pressure but if I had completed this survey a few months ago I think I would have had a lot more positive responses.
This survey is quite time consuming to complete.

Tier of middle management who appear to be "micro managing" well functioning teams.  Members of the team have been re-deployed to help other services which has often put the team under pressure with an impact to team and service.

Too many new mandatory courses being added which are either irrelevant to my role or long winded and time consuming which I find difficult to complete while doing a stressful job with 50% of our staff roles not filled.

Too much emphasis on KPI's.  Patient are not seen as people but rather figures by senior management.  Following a re-structure, the job seems to 'big' for the manager and we do not get adequate supervision.  Senior management do not understand what the 'teams and workers' do - they can be unrealistic when delegating.  Mersey care is a truly miserable place to work!
Too much paper work.
Top down management approach is all too evident.
Trust to be more supportive with team and acknowledge our work ethics and reasoning to improve service.

Unable to complete my masters through the trust as I do not have a [details removed] despite having a degree, CPD at level [number removed] and [timeframe removed] experience.  Unable to self fund as I am [details removed].
Under paid, undervalued, management trying to dodge giving us back pay for a role which has changed drastically over a long time.

Unrelenting and unrealistic targets are driven from community division leaders who don't seem to understand or demonstrate the needs of the population that the team is serving.  Data driven dogma reduces clinical quality, affects clinical decision making - leading to local team managers feeling under pressure to push staff to do more with less time - due to needing to attend to patients with more complexity alongside staffing shortages.  Over last 12 months I have experienced pressures of "see more people quickly" - leading to messages of "discharge quickly" and watering down clinical offer.  Working at speed, offering reduced /less effective clinical intervention and discharging quickly are the elements that appear to be valued by senior community division managers with the organisation.  Some of the changes around safety/risk planning seem to be cumbersome and serve a "tick box" without any care being shown to patients or staff - but is carried under the guise of improving quality..  No exit interviews for those staff who move departments within the organisation misses vital information as to why someone may be leaving, no patterns/themes are investigate.  I'm aware of 8-9 senior staff who have left one IAPT service, myself included, due to bullying behaviour from a senior staff member - but people are too fearful of losing their jobs to raise concerns.  Its felt those senior staff are simply delivering what more senior managers in the service want regarding targets.
Very good survey.
Very recently under a new team leader and have positive hopes for a better team now.

Very unsettling for the team when staff members are continually being moved to help others for long periods of time.  Makes our service feel very undervalued and not appreciated.  Staff morale is at an all time low staff in the service are feeling very deflated over the way team members are moved.  There is a lot of anxiety in the team about potentially being asked to move at very short notice.
We acknowledge this every year, it feels disrespectful for our profession to be classed as 'other AHP' when some are listed.
We are and always have been understaffed.

We are encouraged to innovate and develop new ways of working, but the approval process is long and arduous, we have to submit to senior leadership team in place and then to CCAG and this slows down any new ways of working being launched.  Could there be a quicker process that is less daunting for staff at local level?  We would also like more support with obtaining patient feedback through patient stories and coproduction of services.  Service specs are outdated and need reviewed with opportunities for new service provision and funding opportunities.  There is a disconnect between Operational Leadership teams and those working in more patient safety and quality roles at times.  Lack of admin support also impacts on clinical time spent typing minutes, organising meetings, updating action planners, booking follow up appointments.  Clinical staff do a lot of admin and there is a lot of delegation of work to clinical team from Operations team at times, again impacting on clinical time.  Too much focus on KPIs for response times which are different across teams.  Otherwise there is a really positive culture, staff are respectful to each other and thank each other.  There is a big focus on patient safety.  I really enjoy working at Mersey Care.
We are expected to attend far too many unnecessary meetings, this eats into precious clinical time and results in staff having to do extra work in their own time to catch up.

We are just too overworked.  Increased workload in community nursing but no extra resources put in.  It is too tiring and not enough work/life balance.  I work until late at night and on days off/annual leave days.

We are under a lot of pressure because of very high numbers of referrals in to our service and insufficient clinicians to provide the treatment.  There is no cover for colleagues on maternity leave, or colleagues who are off with long term sickness.  Staff who leave are not replaced.  Management and non clinical staff have suggested that we look at "new ways of working", when really the solution is to ensure we have appropriate staffing levels.  The staff who are in work are at risk from stress and burn out.

We continue to tolerate individuals who are in their position due to their gift of the gab rather than operational or clinical merit, and this is frustrating to watch.  These individuals seem to then get away with recruiting individuals whose faces fit, rather than the most appropriate candidate.  What we tolerate, we promote.  It's a shame because there are some really hard working members of staff who don't get the same recognition.  Also we need to be unafraid to get rid of the coasters - we're not very good at performance managing individuals when they get beyond an 8A - and MARS feels like a reward for them!

We have experienced many changes over recent years, some we have not been consulted on, yet we have not seen the benefit of most of these changes.  When we raise concerns, we are not listened to.  The Management changes that were made, which we were not consulted on, has resulted in a loss of support for the Team and we are now seeing staff leave.  Management over rely on our Team to support others, yet the support is not reciprocal.
We have far to many meetings and mandatory training.

We have recently changed to the 8-8 model.  This has added additional stress within the team.  Having to change base at 5pm till 8pm there is also not enough staff so sometimes your the only 8-8 in your team.  It would work much better if there was always 2 staff members working until 8pm and you could remain at your own base where we feel much safer.  We was told we could split our 1 hour break to two 30 mins but we weren't able to take it at a certain time 8pm is far too late to eat tea if I was able to take my break at 5-5: 30 I'd be able to have my tea at a more reasonable time.

We have very little input from anyone above their level so it can sometimes feel like MSK work in their own silo within the trust.  I feel very privileged to work in this MSK team and I'm very grateful for all that they do for the team.  We've also recently had a much bigger presence and input from some management which has also been really appreciated and they've been able to provide information as to how we fit into the bigger Mersey care picture.

We need an ongoing, recruitment programme, incentives to retain staff.  More inhouse training to continue with knowledge/development.  Value staff that have been with organisation for long time.  Time to complete mandatory training,.  Cascading of important information.  Sometimes feels like 'Open University'.  Liaise with AED for opportunities with training which will benefit, WIC'S/AED'S.
What happened to the word team.  Teams are meant to work together and be proud of what they do.
What is the point of commenting as nothing will happen/change.

When I experienced bullying/harassment it was in a previous position not the current team I am in now.  That is why I left my previous position.  The team leader witnessed it but nothing was done to prevent it from happening again.
When I pressed yes for have I seen any near misses in the last month Patients have come in with wrong blood forms from GPs.

When this asks about managers I do not mean my line manager, my line manager is fantastic, we have been taken over by Mersey Care, since this takeover we have lost most of our staff, we have lots of staff sickness, and the rest of us are looking for other jobs, out of Mersey Care.  Mersey Care are horrendous to work for, they do not care what you are saying how you are feeling, they burn you out.  Far too many managers, it angers me so much, no budget for pay rises especially low paid, however pay these managers who do not even know your job to know how to help, yet they sit day in and day out on teams meetings discussing how to get more work out of you.  Filling out this survey will not do a single thing either!  Maybe you will all go on a teams meeting.

While I feel that my immediate team are very supportive and value our work.  We have undergone changes over the past two years which include reduction of office space/ facilities.  As community staff we are rotated across the city and some teams do not have a base local to the areas they cover.  When working on these teams and under pressure to hit target numbers of patient visits per day we do not have time during a 30 minute lunch break to travel to a health centre that the team use, eat lunch, use facilities and be on time for afternoon appointments.  We need a central base in Liverpool as V7 is in Prescot and out of area for the Liverpool teams.  I have days when I have to eat lunch at supermarket cafes and use their toilet facilities.  Write notes on my laptop in my car.  Which does not feel professional or well supported by the Trust to do my job.  There have been recent incidents of several patients referred for home visits who have weapons such as guns, knives, cross bows and samurai swords in their home.  Through team leads escalating these incidents we have learned more about Weapons in the community policies.  However, it has become clear that other teams within the organisation such as District nurses already knew these patients and had Alerts on their EMIS domain.  The Alerts were not shared across the organisations with all teams who carry out home visits.  It has also been unclear if we have to go to see these patients or are allowed to decline home visits and recommend they attend clinics with more support and security for staff.  There have been a lot of conversations about it and a learning curve for the team.  However, support from senior management, processes to follow and decisions on home visits and how staff feel has been unclear.  Thankfully there has not been an aggressive incident and no one has been harmed.  However, more support and guidance would make staff feel better regarding their roles.
Whilst going through consultation.  I feel that not enough information is being given, causing anxiety and stress.

Whilst I have the opinion that staff working on the 'shop floor' are happy within their teams and work with pts.  And feel supported by their leadership teams, staff at Operational manager level in our place feel that our wings have been clipped since moving into Mersey Care.  There is a lack of autonomy which is extremely frustrating and slows progress down to a snails pace.  It feels like there are far too many processes in place and hurdles to jump over to make changes.  Simple changes to process can take months to be approved and put into place.

Why is no effort made, none at all, to retain staff?  I have suggested several times to different senior management a study be made from the initial advert for a role, to sorting through suitable candidates to initial interview and possible second interview to commencing the role then uniforms, equipment, training etc the costs from the start to becoming ready to fulfil the role.  After all that they leave before 12 months have passed, yet no one asks why?  What can we do?  What can we put in place for you consider staying and for the staff who do stay, why are we supposed to continue year after year in putting up and coping with being always short staffed, picking up shifts on our days off to cover the team to ensure our patients are seen as scheduled, eventually reaching burn out, Yes its real!  But no one ever wants to sort this problem out, we are continually given lip service, borrowing someone from another team, which then leaves them stretched.  I believe this is a suck it up problem which I may add has been going on for the almost [number removed] years I have been in my role.  Only things which do appear to change is the new roles invented for 'the suits' so top heavy, pity the lower level staff don't have this luxury.  You did ask!

Within my current team, a lot of us have young children.  It is really difficult at times due to management not being flexible, meanwhile our current team leader takes their children to school during working hours and nothing is said.  Also, we are now being told when we can and cant use our annual leave.  Currently under threat that we may not get time of in August summer holidays 2025.  We work really hard all year to then be told we cannot have time off with our children during summer holidays.  We have been told we are only allowed x2 band 5 nurses off at once, when there is 17 of us within our team.  Whilst our manager takes all the half terms off as they apparently is not counted in our numbers.  This is causing a lot of staff members to be considering leaving.

Within the Liverpool Adult community physiotherapy team, the team leads do not and have not for the past 12 months received any clinical supervision despite carrying very complex caseloads.  I felt very unsupported and disappointed with the senior leadership team response to a recent incident when a direct threat was made by a patient to a member of my team.  This patient had a variety of weapons in their home and had made a direct threat to a staff member.  This was reported/escalated via the correct channels.  We were advised to return to this patient's home to deliver their physiotherapy treatment by a number of senior managers despite the threat to staff safety that had been highlighted.  This was an incredibly frustrating and concerning response from senior management and we were made to feel that staff safety is not the Trust's priority.  The senior leadership team are so far removed from us as clinicians and the service that we deliver in patient's homes, that they are giving dangerous advice that if followed could have put staff at risk of physical harm.  Our current operational lead has a role that is completely unmanageable, managing too may teams.  As a result tasks such as occupational health referrals for staff are not being completed.

Working for the Children in Care team we require full uniform which I fully respect and appreciate as this is a part of representing our organisation (which I am very proud to work for) however, a lot of Children and young people dislike professionals in uniform and often feel targeted when seeing a professional in uniform and feel "different" from others.  I feel being flexible with uniform would highly appreciated for our children and young people and this will reflect that their voices are behind heard regarding seeing professionals.

Working in the NHS since the pandemic has become extremely challenging.  After [timeframe removed] of service I have never known things to be as difficult as they are for myself and others right now.  I feel that the impact of the pandemic on staff (and the public in general) has been grossly underestimated.  Wellbeing of staff in the NHS has been given a higher profile which is good, but in reality it doesn't have the impact it should.  NHS England, individual Trusts and senior managers can still be preoccupied with statistical outcomes, putting pressure on staff, and therefore cancelling out the wellbeing agendas they seem to be trying to promote.  Burn out in the NHS is a major concern in my opinion and one that needs to be addressed effectively going forward if systems, services and individuals are going to survive into the future.
Workload is ridiculous and it is rarely acknowledged because everyone is experiencing it, so it is now the norm- but it is unsustainable.
Would like the sickness policy to be reviewed.
You could do with separating line managers to more senior management in some of these questions as the answers may be quite different.

Your specialist clinical services admin teams should be managed by the specialist clinical team leaders as they understand the requirements and complexities that the admin job role entails.  Centralised admin teams do not work as they do not understand the complex requirements that are needed for the specialist services.  Being managed from afar is not the way forward it brings so many problems i.e. sick leave, work relationships, guidance, support, work challenges, organisation of work loads team building the list continues.  We have never been able to attend admin team meetings as we can not leave the job for more than half an hour due to the work demands, this is not right.
